For example, I analyzed both of the TSLA trades that Jack and Tony took today and I am not saying I had exactly the same entries and exits but if you analyze the contracts keeping the entry and exit the same I see the following:
For the first trade if you took:
$360 contract = 26% loss
$365 contract = 29% loss
$370 contract = 32% loss
$375 contract = 34% loss
$380 contract = 37% loss
$385 contract = 39% loss
$390 contract = 41% loss
$395 contract = 43% loss
$400 contract = 47% loss
$405 contract = 50% loss

content:
I also analyzed the 2nd TSLA trade and saw a similar trend:
$360 contract = 18% loss
$365 contract = 19% loss
$370 contract = 20% loss
$375 contract = 23% loss
$380 contract = 25% loss
$385 contract = 27% loss

author: RiskManager
content: <@1121621751764369501> Excellent work spotting reversal on AAPL yesterday! Would you share what yo observed in the candles to confirm a reversal and how you determined stop loss? 🙏

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Good Morning Sam - 

To answer your question :  Reversal Confirmation:

I observed a strong bullish rejection around the bottom of a clear demand zone where the price had a previous imbalance. The downward trendline broke as a reversal candle formed with a long lower wick, signaling buyers stepping in.
Following the rejection, a small bullish engulfing candle further confirmed momentum shifting to the upside.
I entered the trade where I labeled "Bought Apple Contracts", as the candles began printing higher lows and the downtrend lost steam.

Stop-Loss Placement:

I placed my stop-loss below the demand zone and the wick of the rejection candle. This aligned with key support and offered a good risk-to-reward ratio while protecting against further downside if the reversal failed.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Hi Gabiruchis,

Thanks for sharing your plan and chart. Looking at QQQ today, I can see why it was tricky—price action looked quite choppy and didn't present a clear A+ setup for clean execution. Here are a few observations to help:

Choppy Structure:

From the chart, QQQ showed a lot of back-and-forth movement, struggling to establish a clear directional bias. This type of market often traps traders on both sides.
Execution Against Levels:

While you planned around the 5-minute levels and targeted the premarket high (536.94), price action failed to show a strong momentum push toward that level after the initial breakout.
Instead, the breakout attempt near the 537 area quickly reversed and formed a Market Structure Shift (MSS) on the 1-minute chart, signaling bearish sentiment.
Buy and Sell Traps:

The "Buy" area was initially a valid trade near trendline support, but price could not sustain higher highs.
The "Sell" reversal shows how critical levels above 537 acted as resistance, and sellers stepped in aggressively.
Lack of A+ Setup:

Overall, today's structure did not offer a clear, high-probability setup.
When price is consolidating or choppy, it’s often better to wait for a clean Break of Structure (BOS), retest confirmation, or stronger momentum.

author: HighVibeLife
content: <@1121621751764369501> Love your recaps/charts. Thank you! Can you speak to why the stop loss on NVDA when it broke the trend line the second time, and not the first time? Would love to understand how you look at holding through vs. sell all in a situation like that. thanks!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Thanks for the kind words! Let me break this down for you:

Why the stop loss on the second break, not the first:
First Candle's Wick Touches the Trendline:

The first candle's wick tested the trendline but did not decisively break it. This can often be interpreted as a false breakdown or a trendline retest.
The wick bouncing off the trendline can indicate indecision, and the trendline still holds as valid support.
Second Candle's Clear Break and Close Below:

The second candle closed below the trendline with no part of the body touching the trendline or 9 EMA. This signifies that the trendline has been broken decisively.
A candle closing below both the trendline and the 9 EMA is a strong bearish signal, warranting an exit to safeguard capital.

Why Hold Through the First Test:
Holding through the first test can be strategic because trendlines often get retested multiple times. Selling too early might lead to missing a potential bounce or continuation in your favor.
The key is to wait for confirmation of the break, which comes when a candle closes below the trendline (with no touch or retest) and undercuts additional support like the 9 EMA.

When to Sell All Contracts:
The trendline break combined with the close below the 9 EMA signals a shift in momentum.
The decision to sell all contracts was based on the lack of support from both the trendline and the EMA, indicating that the bullish thesis no longer held.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Not sure of there's right/wrong answer for this

But if your ticker fails to go higher than yHOD, then you could use it as BnR level for going short, however I'd wait until it takes yLOD for having a better quality setup, because then you will trading internal liquidity (scalping) instead of waiting for external liquidity and a bigger move

And you need to be careful as the 1min/5min could look like a failed attempt but the 15min/1hr could be telling a different story (Price is fractal)

This is exactly what Jack said in the Playbook and A+ setups video about aligning all the timeframes for your move. Or said differently, you don't short in the smaller timeframe a ticker than has an uptrend in the HTF

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@1025807331327475863> 

Great question! Comparing your second chart and my first one, here’s how I’d approach the scenarios you outlined:

Scenario 1 (Green Arrow):
If NVDA filled the gap, broke the 5-minute opening range, retested the bottom line of the opening range, and then pushed up:

Yes, I would consider entering after the retest and confirmation of strength (e.g., a bullish candle closing above the retest level).
This setup aligns with gap-fill momentum, which often leads to a continuation if volume supports it.
Stop Loss (SL): Just below the retest level or slightly under the gap-fill zone to account for noise.
Scenario 2 (Red Arrow):
If NVDA broke the top of the 5-minute opening range, retested, and then started selling:

No, I wouldn’t enter here. The initial breakdown of the top of the range would signal weakness, especially if it couldn’t hold the breakout level.
Entering short after the breakdown could work for a different strategy, but for me, I’d wait for further confirmation that the sellers are in control before considering a trade in this direction.
Entry at 8:19 (Orange Arrow):

I entered because NVDA was forming a bullish triangle (as shown in my chart), signaling consolidation with the likelihood of a breakout. The price was respecting key levels like the premarket high and hourly resistance.
The breakout was accompanied by increasing volume, confirming strength.
SL for this trade: Below the last low of the triangle (around $138.51 on my chart), allowing the price room to breathe while managing risk effectively.
The primary difference is context—whether NVDA respects its key levels (gap-fill, opening range, premarket high) and how volume supports the move. Let me know if that helps clarify!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Trade Recap – QQQ (March 6, 2025)
Morning Chop: Multiple BNRs failed, no clean setups, stayed out.
Entry: Short after break & retest of key level.
Trend Confirmation: Strong rejection, followed the downtrend.
Trade Management: Scaled out at TP1 & TP2, respected trendline.
Exit: Closed all remaining contracts when price broke the PYA (Protect Your Asset) trendline.
Key Takeaways:

✅ Avoided early chop – waited for a solid setup.
✅ Break & retest gave conviction for the short.
✅ Trendline guided trade – locked profits before reversal.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Overview of Both Strategies:
My Strategy: I waited for the stock to clearly break above a crucial price level before entering, which typically signals that the stock might continue to rise. This is safer because it confirms the stock's upward momentum. I set a stop-loss close to my entry point to minimize potential losses and took profits as the price went up, adjusting my stop-loss to secure gains.

Your Strategy: UG (your)  entered the trade just after the stock retested an important level, without waiting for further confirmation of an upward trend. This approach can be riskier because it's uncertain whether the stock will keep rising after the retest. The stop-loss strategy wasn’t clearly defined, which might expose the trade to larger potential losses. You also entered after a significant price increase, which sometimes means the stock could pull back as traders start taking profits.

content: At <@1121621751764369501> , any feedback for this trade... I waited for 5 min Opening range and had a good retest along with nice downclose candle... any issue with the thesis here? Or issue with the timing... this is my guess that probably the market was moving up and I entered at wrong time... also the 5 min range was not in the favor as well...

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Your thesis was solid in terms of waiting for the 5-minute opening range and entering on the retest with a strong downclose candle. However, the execution timing was likely off.


Market Context Issue: The broader market looked to be moving up, which means you were taking a countertrend trade. This added risk to your setup.


5-Minute Range: As you pointed out, the range wasn’t in your favor. If the range is tight, there’s limited room for price to move cleanly.


Inside Ranging: I personally dislike trading when price is stuck inside a defined range (between key highs/lows) because:
There’s no clear breakout or momentum.

Price tends to chop around, making it hard to get a clean directional move.

Stop-loss placements become trickier due to unpredictable wicks.

Possible Fix: Consider waiting for a cleaner breakout or signs of market trend alignment before taking an entry.
Overall, great thought process, just need to refine entry timing and market alignment!

author: Tito Frescado
content:
Overview:
FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. I thought that since AMZN was above YDH so early in the day with a strong uptrend behind it on all timeframes that it might continue. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill. Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 

Setup Details:
Entry: FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. AMZN showed a strong uptrend on all timeframes. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill.
Position Size: Discuss the position size taken and why. Mention any adjustments made for risk management.
Exit: Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 
Profit/Loss: Lost 20 dollars on the trade. On second look, the was perhaps too optimistic trade on FMOC day when you expect the trade to stay within the PDR. Perhaps my entry was too aggressive because although the hammer was red and might have worked on another day, it was FMOC day. I should have waited for a green confirmation candle close above the red hammer that I entered on. It would have kept me out of the trade.

Rating: ⭐ [5 of Stars]
⭐ 1 Star: Although the setup was good. I should have waited for more confirmation that it wanted to continue above YDR.
Key Takeaways:
Lesson 1: Expect price to stay within YDR on FOMC day before 2:00. Wait for price to reach YDH or YDL looking for a quick scalp in the opposite direction
Lesson 2: Look for solid confirmation before entering a trade on FMOC day.
Missed Opportunities: No. the was a retest of the top of the daily gap but not enough room to move before hitting resistance at YDH.
Other Factors: FOMC

author: 305 Trader
content: Go low in contracts, just as I am doing, perfect your entries and refine your trade rules. When  you prove to yourself that your gains are picking up go with more contracts. Some traders here are ok with risk and admit easily they can lose. but this takes time. It seems your fear is that you do not trust your own trading rules. Review them. author: Luke
content: what’s up <@1351067135899734046>, the narrative is on the 15min timeframe, and the entry and risk is on the 1min or 5min. I personally don’t take a lot of entries/manage risk on the 5min timeframe; I use the 1min timeframe majority of the time. But when it comes to the reaction around the key level, I wait for a strong 5min candle close off of that level showing the buyer or sellers stepping in. So if you’re entering a Break and Retest on the 5min timeframe, wait for a 5min candle close below your stop loss, and if you’re entering on the 1min timeframe wait for a candle close below your stop loss on the 1min. Hopefully that makes sense. The narrative is on the 15min, but the entries are on the 1min or 5min.

author: Kam
content: if trading options, have to accept some asymmetrical risk on both sides because of the impact of greeks on how the options are priced. Especially with the IV as high as it is on options pricing in the markets right now due to elevated volatility. This looks like the TSLA trade from a couple days ago - if trade is going sideways or against you the IV+theta will kill the options value very fast. The flip side to this is when you get a move in your favor, can make exponential / asymmetric returns also. remember, options value acccelerates the further price moves away fro your strike price. in this case, you didn't really get that aggressive acceleration on the down move - sort of chopped down. unfortunately on the move back up to your stop, the price did move fast in the wrong direction

content: HI. I reviewed this set up just now. This set up served very well the 1st 15 min candle ORB strategy but to the d/s.  It did retest even twice the low. Now, only thing I can say against that CALL was that on the Daily chart the stock was overextended in relationship to the ADR of the previous 9 days. Context is so important. The stock opened up following a huuge gap, so I would have wanted to wait and observe what direction it would pick. There is this rule that I try reminding myself that says: Do not buy stock that is overextended to either side.  Oscar, buy CALLS in discount area and go short in Premium area. This simple yet tried and true notion can save you from FOMO and from doing a bad entry. Everything is possible, yes, but the odds are not in our favor when we buy when the stock or index is overextended.

author: Ruslan /ruˈslan/
content:
Hi Kuba, I think I have the same issue as you, and maybe my experience can help. I also trade with just one contract (due to a small account) and often end up with small profits because I chickened out and exited too early — or I hesitated to sell, price dropped, and I took a loss instead of securing a small green.
I asked <@1014389514141184074> for advice on the best strategy for trading with a single contract — maybe it'll help you too. He said: “Set your TP at 1:1.5 RR, and once price goes past it, turn that level into your stop-loss and see if it continues.” Though I see you're already going for 1:2 RR, which is great.
But here's something I realized this past week: it’s not really about  "chickened out" vs "had the balls", it’s about trading a single contract with no ability to scale out, and I'll explain why. Despite my small account, I decided to try trading two contracts this week — and man, it’s a whole different game. Trading before and after this shift feels like night and day.
That inner conflict is gone — no more one voice saying “hold for more profit” while the other says “just take what you can before it turns red.” Now I calmly wait for TP1, take partial profit, or get stopped out — either way, it feels in control. And when you lock in some gains at TP1, it’s so much easier to let the rest ride toward TP2 with a peaceful mind.
Another great tip I got from <@801169196015943731> — which I now use — is after scaling out at TP1, move your stop to breakeven. That way you protect your profits and keep your risk low.
Sure, sometimes price reverses right away and it’s a small red — but I liked what <@1014389514141184074> said yesterday on trade review — that’s still a win. Because you followed your plan, didn’t lose control, and didn’t blow half your account 😄 We're not aiming for a 100% win rate anyway.
So my advice: try adding that second contract. It changes everything.

author: Kam
content:
Took a pretty lousy trade on PLTR this morning and wanted to share my review. Open to any thoughts or other feedback! 

PLTR – 6/26/25
Setup: BnR 1m OB Continuation + 5m OR Retest
Result:  Loss (–1R)
Confidence on Entry: 3/5
Sizing: Base Size

What Happened:
Strong opening drive in PLTR with RS and news catalyst. Took an anticipatory reclaim entry off the 1m OB after visual strength near prior pivot, but skipped confirmation and entered on candle stretch. Market context was improving, 5m candle looked like a nice hammer, but pullback was steep and PLTR can be trappy. Trade hit stop shortly after entry. Earlier missed trade off PMH may have been a factor as well as pass on NVDA OB play prior day.

Classification:
False Form – Anticipatory Entry 
Good structure idea, poor entry execution. Trade plan shifted from OB pullback to reclaim without re-evaluation.

Primary Mistake:
Premature Entry – No Reclaim Confirmation

What Worked:
- Avoided forced dip buys in semis
- Honored stop and respected system
- Solid structure read on RS and catalyst

Needs Work:
– Entry discipline on reclaims
– Slight emotional residue from passing on NVDA prior day and PMH retest at the open
– OB sequence clarity and reclaim filters need reinforcement

author: Jatin
content: Hi, I am looking to get some feedback on the trade I took this morning. Entered in at 9:57 candle with confluences with QQQ and SMH with 2 cons. 1st profit target at HOD and was then following strictly order blocks for continuation with the last con but stopped out at 10:07 candle. I did not wait for the candle closure below $155.60 but exited on the wick as I wanted to avoid any losses that was a mistake I realize. Anything else that I could have done differently. Any feedback is much appreciated.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Jatin - this is a tricky one for me since it's lower timeframe bearish, but HTF bullish. I didn't take this trade, but here's how I hypothetically would have managed it:

The 155 area was the reclaim level after the 5m hammer into the PDH level so once we get a candle close above that level I'd draw out the full OB and use that as structural invalidation. I'd have taken profit like you at HOD. That nasty wick held that OB - and after taking profits at HOD, the trade is de-risked so if it comes back it comes back, and stop out for BE or small loss depending on your size. I probably would have sold into new high pops at 156.5, but these setups usually work fast and hit PMH quickly. So once it stalled out, I probably would have been cautious when got back under the HOD pivot and 1m/5m OB to stop out of trailers. 

There's different ways to trail though - I tend to use OB's and structure to trail, but others like <@1121621751764369501> use trendlines and EMAs. <@1014389514141184074> uses a 5m timer! Just have to find your style!

Easy to play armchair QB with hindsight bias i know 😂  But that's my 2c, thanks for asking - this was a good thought exercise!

I know Tony & Jack took this trade cos they were just talking about it in Traders Lab - so definitely check out Tony's trade review of this tonight!

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
As part of my Performance Coaching Program, let me share one of my favorite tools to stay sharp while I’m in a trade: a simple 5-minute timer.

I started using this because I noticed that when I wasn’t checking in regularly, emotions could sneak in and take over my decisions — hesitation, fear, frustration, or even overconfidence. So I added this timer to help me stay present, focused, and objective during the trade.

Every 5 minutes, it reminds me to check in with my position — not to overreact, but to stay connected and aware. I ask myself:
    •    Is my stop loss or invalidation level still valid?
    •    Are we getting closer to my targets?
    •    What’s happening with price action and market structure?
    •    Is there any news, correlation, or divergence that could affect the trade?

This timer is now one of my tools to overcome emotional decision-making. It’s like a little coach tapping me on the shoulder every few minutes saying:
“Hey, are you still managing this trade intentionally?”

Try it out — you might be surprised how much clarity and confidence it can bring to your sessions.

author: Weezy
content: <@1014389514141184074> <@1121621751764369501> Can you help me understand what I did wrong in this trade—aside from stepping away for two minutes right before my stop was hit and the price kept going?

author: 305 Trader
content: hi. Its called Loss aversion, your emotions are protecting your need to be right, and you try hard to be right cause admitting loss is failure in our wiring. But being a trader is about accepting loss from the get-go. You can only control your loss, the market gives you wins when it wants to. This is an explanation I repeat myself over and over as I struggled with your same problem for years. No adding though, but loss aversion yes. Watch few videos on trading mindset in youtube, I know one great channel on psychology of trading but its in spanish. Two things, try mechanical stop losses while you work your psychology, this means do only A+ set ups, two trades at most, and cut down in size.

author: 305 Trader
content: ok, remember this is a mind battle. If you have found your edge in this community of Jack and Tony, that is number 1. Once you found your edge and make sure you have one cause you only need one strategy, not many, submit it to sample 20 trades with a mechanical stop loss perhaps 10-20% of your contracts value (depending on contracts size) being your risk. If after 20 trades you have been always stopped out, then your edge needs to be revised or your entry timing adjusted. Journal this. Also, what matters is your profit factor, not your win ratio.

Always follow Risk Management rules.
Always risk no more than 1-2% of an account.
Always have my STOP LOSS in place.
Always trade 1 contract ONLY.
Always make no more than 1-2 trades a day.
Always STOP trading after 1 losing trade.
Always avoid FOMO, GREED, REVENGE trades at all cost.
Always track my trades and emotions by journaling.

author: Royal191
content:
Hey <@268284125897687041> At a high level view, I would say slow down and focus on 1-2 trades per day. If you took 2 Ls there is no need to continue and possibly end up with 4 or 5 Ls. Just call it a day and review what went wrong and come back the next day with a fresh view. Overtrading and revenge trading is a big reason why traders are not profitable because your losing days become bigger than your winning days.
Based on what you said it seems like you might be chasing trades or anticipating moves. You should spend some time and refine your system rather than trying out different strategies or entry models. (if you haven’t watched the accelerator videos yet, I would highly recommend you do so).
Also based on your charts it looks like some of your trades are kinda taken in the middle of no where, away from your levels with the exception of  the TSLA entry. On the TSLA trade by the way I can see a V shape recovery on your chart before you entered short.  You said it yourself on the TSLA trade you were anticipating the move. Watch the accelerator modules. Try to plan your trades in the pre-market and focus on those and wait for entries/reaction at your key levels. If you need help coming up with a plan for the day and main watches. Join Jack’s pre-market prep, over time you will learn how to read the HTF, how to draw key levels and much more. Check out the old trade reviews from Jack and Tony. Easiest way to learn what they look for and when they enter trades. https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1219022089252503632 and https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1222648559682457650

author: Sean
content:
Update: I was able to successfully stick to my risk management plans and continuously improve it throughout the month. 

Some statements that have helped me stick to the plan are:

- There is no certainty in trading—only probabilities. 
- Acknowledge that a loss is simply one outcome in a long series of trades. 
- I can always look to reenter the trade if something sets up

Plans for Next Month: Same thing as last Month, Respect my stop loss. Search for points of invalidation in my trades before entering and respect that stop no matter what. 

Hopefully, this helps anyone struggling with the same challenges. I'm right there with you. We'll get there. One trade at a time. Size wisely, place stops with purpose, and stay disciplined. Consistency beats perfection. Keep showing up — we’ve got this. 🥂 💪

author: Ruslan /ruˈslan/
content:
Guys, I want to share my crappy day because of my own stupidity. The story is actually kind of funny 🙂
I took 1 TSLA 302.5 Put 0DTE contract, held it the whole time while the price was falling, and as soon as it reversed after 298, I couldn’t sell—like hypnotized—waiting for it to go back down, all the way until it climbed up to 301.5, and only then did I lock in a $30 profit, even though I could’ve made a few hundred bucks.
And then classic: I immediately jumped into a revenge trade and locked in a $70 loss.
I thought I’d learned to get out on a mental stop-loss (sometimes it’s tough mentally, especially after red days), but I didn’t expect myself to hesitate and not take the profit.
Just wanted to share the reflection 🙂
To everyone who’s green today—respect. And to those who aren’t—also, because we’re still here and still trying to get better.

author: Ruslan /ruˈslan/
content:
Agreed! Honestly, it’s really tough trading a small account and sticking to risk management (5% risk of the whole account). I tried the 3-contract setup, but with FOTM. When price moves in your favor, you’re barely green, and when you hit the stop-loss the premium collapses hard—so the account just shrinks.
So I decided to try something else: one OTM contract, just a single contract. That has its own quirks too, but I’ll say this: I’m really happy with how the price moves. So I’m thinking I’ll try to grow the account on this method until I can take three 1OTM contracts without risking blowing the account on red days.

author: Royal191
content: This trade was a scalp based on the relative strength TSLA was showing. Gapped up in the pre market above a key HTF zone, retested and held the zone.  Main TP on this trade was the orange line, but I wanted to see if we would have continuation to the blue line above based on the strength after the retest. At the time of entry Qs and spy were consolidating near the opening range so if they had moved to the upside, TSLA would have likely been the one leading the way up. After TSLA rejected and closed below the orange line I wasn’t interested in looking for more upside so I stopped out at the white mark. When TSLA closed below that orange line spy was breaking to the downside at the same time. So with spy breaking to the downside with a gap fill below, it was likely TLSA would come back down after rejecting that HTF level. Tony took the same or similar entry and made 44k so his trade review should be able to explain things better.

content: hi <@1238566536579514509> I’ve been fighting that demon of Loss aversion as well. It ruins everything accomplished. I set up a limit order for my initial stop loss now. Then as the trade takes off in my favor hopefully, I notice with options that hard stop loss becomes less in value for the same invalidation level, so thats pretty nice and encouraging to do it. author: Anne
content:
I made many mistakes today as I was emotionally driven:
I entered very early (which I have to stop doing) (9:33)on NVDIA with Jack. Next mistake on this trade: I did not wait for the confirmation candle to close. So I lost on this trade (-130$) when I exited at 9:55. I was already very unhappy with myself and felt the urge for revenge trading. I did not do it immediately, but my next two trades were not done according to the process focused trading I learn here in this group.
Then at 9:48 I entered HOOD on its immediate retest of 5 min high. This time I waited for the confirmation candle to close. I was red with that for the next 8 minutes which made me emotions become much stronger then my reason. I scaled out as soon as I was green, the last con I sold at 10:03. In regard to the process this moves are not good at all because I had no candle close below my stop loss. If I had been stronger than my emotions I would have won my loss back while HOOD moved up from this minute on.
While I was fighting with my emotions I entered TSLA listening to Tony. The price action was strong, so I got a bad entry. It moved in my risk area for ten mins which I could not digest. When it moved up I exited quickly for not to take another loss today. Especially these TSLA losses are heavy. And then I saw the stock move up really nice.

author: Royal191
content: Hi Anne, glad to see you are able to identify the issue so quickly. Now you can make a plan/rules to avoid this happening in the future. Revenge trading is one of those things that will wreck your mental capital and your actual capital. Especially revenge trading with names like TSLA or META. If you take a bad trade, try to step away from the screens for a few mins and clear your head. If you decide to come back and look for another trade you have to treat the next trade as its own trade and trade it without the emotions from the prev trades. Once you have the prev loss in your mind it will be easier to make mistakes like taking profits too early or even holding too long to make up the loss and letting a green trade turn red.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Hi Max. Thanks for sharing your trades which lost, I think is brave of you to decide to do self accountabilty on your losses and to share. We learn a lot from our losses and other people’s.,I took the time to draw some thoughts and took screenshots of your charts. Then I draw where I would enter, not knowing anything about HTF and a narrative on those tickers, just purely intraday patterns from the chart.  I trade patterns. 

I know it may be a different style for you. I think the market always form patterns like a gradual ascending (long) staircase, or a gradual descending (short) staircase. These patterns repeat over and over intraday but it needs time to form. 

Trading the 1st 15 mins of market open in my opinion requires a very strong HTF narrative formed, a strong understanding of the stock and a good gut for being ok with accepting larger losses.  And this is because the market has not formed patterns yet, its only reaction to pre market and gaps. 
I trade differently. This is what I watch for, I follow a staircase formation which breaks price structure along  key levels of confluence and only act when a key level is respected as support or resistance to go in continuation. I think Neto trades this way more often than the other coaches. Its a matter of choice and backtesting. Very important this b/t part. 

Lastly, one advice, do not do initial mental stop loss. This is only for traders who have an extraordinary emotional management achieved and a rigurous discipline aka Jack or Tony.  I am not exaggerating. Our job is to defend our profits. The market’s is to provide the wins. 

Here is my screenshots of where I would have liked to enter safely.

author: jav
content: Hey Max, I am certainly not someone to be giving advice but somethings I have learned and advice I have been given that you may find useful. In regards to a mental stop or a hard stop, with a hard stop if the candle hits your stop area your going to be stopped out of the trade, which is why Tony and Jack always say to wait for candle closure. I used to always use a hard stop but I would mostly get stopped out and then the stock would take off in the way I wanted it to, which can be very nerve racking. I am not sure what your set up was on those 2 trades but Tony advises you to stay with one set up when you are a new trader, whether that be the 5 minute opening range, premarket or previous day and just stay with whichever your most comfortable with. I personally only trade the 5 minute opening range break. I also switched to a 5 minute chart because trading off a 1 minute chart I found to be super stressful. The only con to a 5 minute chart when waiting for candle closure is if you get a big candle the opposite of your trade your stop is going to be much lower than you wanted it to be, but that has only happened once in TSLA.  I hope you found something useful in this and good luck in your trading journey.

author: 305 Trader
content: Setting up the correct stop loss (where the trade invalidates) at start is a real art guys and sticking to it with a mechanical stop loss is the only way to create a good habit of accepting the risk of trading. No hope, just a mechanical trade plan implemented with discipline. Mental stop losses do not help us create good habits. They are helpful when the disciplined trader has become the operator. Before this, mental stop losses are a demon for me.

author: Anne
content:
Very bad day today regarding PnL (-1988$), good day regarding "respect the process". I saw the trades through until they hit SL. Than I added a fourth trade only because I am still on paper and I want to use this state to try out what comes up in my brain. As I am a very bad rule holder I decided last week to implement only one rule after the other: First I did risk managment (don't stay in the trade after candle close beneath SL), next is: only exit when SL or PT is hit. In other words: don't look at the PnL, look at the process. Well, today that wasn't a nice thing to do but I don't feel really bad with such a loss on paper anymore. That means I don't identify my personal value with the outcome of a trade anymore. 
The fourth trade I took was out of the "take only three trades a day" rule but I allowed it due to that I am still on paper and I did not implement that rule yet. That rule will be the last one that I will implement while being on paper trading. The most important rule for me now is: Stay in the trade until it hits PT. My wining stats are good, I just exited too early up to now because I rather took a few dollars win then loosing a trade. This is going to change!

author: 305 Trader
content: Today, having a mechanical stop loss and accepting that an outcome can be a stop out is what preserved my capital successfully. I had two controlled losses. A month ago, I would have been the "hopeful trader" and possibly I could have wiped out my small account. But my trade plan got activated in regards to the stop loss and it was executed. The 2nd trade was applying a rule of the market that says: "after a failed signal, a stronger one to the opposite direction follows". Only problem was that today is an atypical day. So, because atypical reactions can happen, a stop loss that is clear and accepted must be in place.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi Momo, I like what Neto says. I am paying a lot of attention to the subj of Stop loss. I think that is the break point between a profitable trader and a limping one. For your metrics I think is important to decide which system you will use for the next 50 trades. This way you can measure your Profit factor (not the win rate). If your chosen S/L gives you a high profit factor then you know it works. I remember what I heard yesterday in a mindset video, two large stop losses can take out my profits of 10 trades, but 10 small stop losses can be taken out by two good wins, so what is preferable?

author: Anne
content:
Today I traded 0DT and I won't do that in the near future again. The way the loose worth is too much for me to digest right now. 
I lost 84$ and I did so on entering PLTR (-59$) as it already was very much overextended. So this was fomo again that took me there. Then I tried to make up for it (again revenge trading) and I entered on NVDA on the retest of a downclose candle at 10:11 just after the break of PDH. I entered on the next confirmation candle and stayed in that trade for 50 mins until it finally brok below PDH. It was a low probability trade and I could have exited as the structur broke (after 34 mins). But I wanted to stay in the trade until SL broke so I stayed and made a loss of 24$.
So everything allright with the loss, just doing these bad executions has to change. Sitting on my hands (overcoming fomo) is the next skill I plan to learn after holding through to PT/SL (change focus from PnL to process).

author: 305 Trader
content: awesome brother, observation is great to sharpen it like a samarai katana. 🙂 My feedback is that on the 1st circle you've got a solid liquidity grab and rejection of the KL which was at that point forming a lower high. Since that is the POI for your 2nd circle, and while trading options we want the most momentum, I think is important to always assume there will be a pullback after a breakout to that lower high because that is the ChOCH confirmation you were awaiting. So I would take my entry after a pullback which took place 4 candles later and adjust my stop loss accordingly. also interesting to see what the 5 min TF was saying.

author: Anne
content: You are right, Oscar. My PLTR trade actually wasn’t a bad setup. On the 5 min timeframe I wouldn’t have been stopped out and after that dip at 11:15 it moved up nicely. I shouldn’t have bought 0DTE and I should have managed the trade on the 5 min timeframe. I would have been profitable then 😊

author: 305 Trader
content: <@1047881373370818610> , 0DTE forces you to have a quality of an entry. You still can have a narrow stop loss as you did. But in my opinion, as someone who only trades 0DTE, you need a clear confirmation candle and a strong set up. You did not have either one at your entry: No displacement at near ATH, and bars with long wicks which tell you market is indecisive. Still it would have worked out but with a larger stop loss such as below the Low of the green candle that brokeout your KL. This is part of the problem of weak set ups, that they need a large stop loss for them to work.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi <@1398013674194468996> great question I often asked myself. It depends on how much attention and focus you need to take a trade. Think of it as a High-jump jumper in a track & field competition. Even though they are top performers, the tournament requires that they take a break in between their three attempts to make their best high jump. If you alread won in 1x trade, I would take a break and reflect on what happened, drink water. Only after my break I may come back to the charts. But now, think of actually the downside. and this one is critical. What do you do if you lose on the 1st trade? This is an even more interesting challenge, most people including some coaches here will be tempted to revenge trade and they will actually take more trades, with that loss in mind. This is a bigger mistake or offense to your own trading plan and system.

author: Luke
content: that’s awesome brother, you can never go wrong tracking all the data from your trades. Another thing I just noticed is there wasn’t much displacement on that trade, and you were inside the previous day’s range. In my playbook, I only take trades with that little of displacement off Previous Day High or levels outside the previous day’s range where I have a strong bias and higher time frame thesis of it working. Like PDH retest on AMD today is a good example of little displacement but makes sense to be more aggressive. In my opinion, there’s no reason to be that aggressive on a Premarket High retest inside the previous day’s range. Hopefully that makes sense🫡

author: 305 Trader
content: <@293563891345129472>  Notice the difference between a price consolidation or also “ranging” and a pullback. This to me is the main reason the 1st entry failed. 2nd reason, little displacement on the 1st entry. If your RvR measured to TP1 is 1:2 that increases your probs of a win big big time. You will have fewer trade set ups but you will win good.

author: 305 Trader
content: <@1303664078404718632> Hey man, thats unfortunate you had that loss. When trading 1 min TF you need extra confirmation, probably the opening of a 3rd candle in your favor even, but that is a personal preference. Take a look at the notes I made for you. That downward trend was not stable -yet- at the time of your entry, because there was a ChOCH prior that later passed on a check to some of the traders at your entry, not just you. I  started journaling the impact of ChOCH in a trend for riding a continuation, and I am finding that ChOCH disrupts a continuation of the trend.

author: terenceght
content:
IMO, the setup is indeed very nice but the context is wrong (i was looking at that exact same setup at that time) 

Look at QQQ at 9:46, its bouncing at key level on HTF. Zoom out to 15min TF and u will realise that QQQ is at ‘resistance turned support’ level. Any reversal on QQQ will invalidate your short setup on TSLA, which is exactly what happened

author: terenceght
content:
Yes correct. Thats what i always do. Context n reference towards indices are very important. 
U might miss out on some winning trades but u will avoid getting into bad trades with wrong context (like what happened today on TSLA)
If u only focus on setups then it makes u a pattern day trader.

The price for TSLA was in consolidation going by the emas and the high time frames along with the price action and has been for a while now for a good part of the week. The setup itself honestly wasn't bad per but the price was above the pre market low really. 

What this was if you marked it out was a retest of the previous close from a few days ago I think like 2 or three days ago.  

The setup itself honestly was valid but the context wasn't the best. 

QQQ was ranging in a way where the price action itself was super choppy and TSLA was moving in a similar way.

I see two ways you could have possibly traded this: the first would be trading the order block / the previous downclosed candle ie the reversal with a entry at 10:29 play the rejection of the 5 minute high and the order block we made earlier.

There is also a retest of the previous close at 10:40 as well but you would have to be aggressive to get the rr you were looking for. <@1141348957130334318>

author: Kam
content:
No worries, glad it helped. Love that attitude, keep it up! 

Definitely backtest the 5m closures so that you can find what works for your style/system. 

Waiting for 5m closes can widen the stops sometimes and kill the R/R so stop placement is pretty critical and keep time of day top of mind. 

For stops, focus on the level that's being reclaimed (if trading a reclaim). I used to be really bad  at reclaims when I first joined this room (ask <@1062934985109160057> 😂), but now they're becoming one of my best setups so definitely keep practicing! 

Generally from my data it's ok for a level to be retested after a reclaim, but these setups should move pretty quickly so don't want to give too much room.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi. Trading in 1 min TF gives quite often false signals for the B&R method. So it is essential that you observe higher Lows stacking up prior your entry to counter false signals. See my screenshot. Yu also had lower lows (not higher lows) prior your failed entry.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Good observation. I have also observed that Jacks SL are pretty wide often. I do not plug in in the live trading. What I suggest is, focus on what is working for you. Look for set ups with 1:3 RvR that actually have a small stop loss, one you can afford and be comfortable with especially because it can be hit.  One that will not put you in disarray and cause to go crazy about the loss. Because a loss is perhaps more certain than a win in this business. 
Jack trades big league and his tolerance for a stop loss (mentally and account wise), is not the same as yours and mine.

author: Das Wookie
content:
I broke a number of my rules today with my trades... not all of my rules, but quite a bunch of them:
- Don't trade after 11 - I couldn't resist the afternoon downturn to go short on PLTR and GOOG
- Don't revenge trade - I lost my first entry trying a trade with RGTI, so I exited the position, played a Uno Reverse card on my position and immediately re-entered, and lost again! -$4k on both. Doggie! That sucked!
- Don't chase! DON'T F-ING CHASE! Got it! - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't force trades - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Sit on your hands and WAIT for your entry - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't flit from one trade to another - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Max of 3 daily options trades... I entered in on 11 trades in total today
- Don't over trade - See max 3 trades
- If I do well on either of my initial 2 trades, and I'm green for the day... you're done! - I only had the two trades on TSLA and INTC, but I got greedy and wanted more. It worked out, but it shouldn't have. I got lucky that it worked out, but I SHOULD have just taken the initial win and shut down for the day and protected my wins. With the RGTI trade, I nearly went negative on all of my initial TSLA trade gains
- Capture gains at key levels and level out of position! - I ended up losing a potential $33k because I didn't take profit at a key level
- Don't move your stop! didn't exit at my initial stop, and then moved my stop, twice, trying to recapture the potential unrealized gain instead of just capturing the trade where my initial stop was set.

author: Das Wookie
content:
I generally am not, no. Sometimes I'll have it on in the background, just so I can listen in on what the boys are doing, but for the most part I'm doing my own thing. I trade more than B&R, and watch more symbols... so if the Mag 7 aren't doing anything interesting, and my thesis for the day ended up wrong... well then I'll just go wherever my watchlist leads me... and where one of my setups appears.

Today, it would have been VERY difficult to have not printed if you were still paying attention after 11, and the bottom of the bucket fell out for the next 2 hours. Today was an amazing velocity trade to the downside!!! This sorta day doesn't come around very often... at least not without some tweet from the pumpkin king that causes the entire market to panic. I was in a $40k trade last time that happened, and my jaw hit the floor!!! "Holy ****!!!! What the... What just happened!??? Babe! Pull up the news. Did the pentagon just explode or a dirty bomb go off in NY or LA????" as I watched TRILLIONS of dollars vaporize before our very eyes. I didn't have the emotional stability to cut my loss RIGHT THEN and switch to PUTS. I was too locked in on my CALL trade, and didn't want to cut the position... thinking "This HAS to come back! This is insane! What is the market doing?!!! C'Mon!!! Come back up! Please! PLEASE!!!" where as now, I'd just cut it. RIGHT then. Without Mercy! "It's just numbers." and these days, I'd just SCRAM the trade. That's also the basis for some of my rules. We all establish our own rules. Sometimes I break mine, but I do my level best to only do it for a VERY good reason. Sometimes tho, I slip up... like with my two RGTI trades today which was the basis of violating more than half of the rules I broke today. Of course, had I NOT broken that rule, and tried to chase a win there... I'd have already been out of the market and entirely missed this afternoons drop because I'd have been out of the market already and on to do other things.

author: Das Wookie
content: In the interest of sharing, I had an absolutely HORRIFIC trading day today. I lost my first two trades for the day, and should have followed my rule and walked away right then. Instead, I chased, and entered a trade without a proper setup entry JUST because it looked like it might be good. I sized WAY up to 400 contracts too try and make up for my loss. Turns out tho, there were NO options traders on that symbol. The Bid/Ask Spread was MASSIVE with a $3.50 spread, but I didn't notice until I went to exit. There were NO buyers. NONE!!! I pulled up the Time & Sales, and I thought the data connection was broken. I pulled up history, and I saw some trades of mostly 1 contact, a few for 2 contracts, a rare 5 or 10, and then my 400 contacts buy. There we no other options trades. I couldn't exit! Well I could, but with THAT spread, it was BRUTAL. I listed the limit sale at the ASK, and nothing happened for 30 seconds. I dropped my price by $0.10. Then again. And again. And again... and And AND!!! I finally sold my position after dropping my ASK $3.40 when it FINALLY sold. I went from being up and being green for the day, and turning that into a loss of $140k. I was DEVASTATED. I couldn't believe I got myself into that position. I found a stock that was moving, and did well... and destroyed myself. I did a few trades after that, but there was ZERO chance of being green. I was just TRYING to limit the damage. I ended up -$137k for the day. It's not the end of the world for me. I'm still green for the week, but it's embarrassing, emotional, and gravely stupid you have done. But it is what it is! So... There's my last 6 days of trading. It seems like people only ever post their gains or complain about losses. I traded emotionally. I chased. I kept increasing size to try and get back to even. I did ask if this on a 0DTE Friday of all days!!! I was, an idiot! So, I'm falling on my sword here.

author: Das Wookie
content: I can't believe I did this, but I did. I've never been trapped by such a unbelievably massive spread before! I did EVERYTHING wrong today.

author: Das Wookie
content:
I try to be up most of the time as well with my outlook. Yeah, I'm still green for the week, and it was one week I'll remember for sure! I shouldn't have traded today. I felt self applied pressure to pull a big win again today.

The market wasn't having it tho.

author: 305 Trader
content: ok. Mike Y, I think you may be trading in a complicated system, though if it works for you keep doing it. Now, the brackets Limit orders may not be suitable for options because, if you are right the top limit order can limit you as the options contract value grows exponentially once it hits ITM. On the other hand, a stop limit order to a loss can be 15 cts in the first go but then it may go down to 30 cts on the 2nd retracement while the underlying is the same $ value. So take a look at this. I think is a better way to manage risk when you know what is your max loss per trade and per day and adjust accordingly your contract size AND, filter out the RvR more suitable. This latter will keep you away from trading what does not meet your trading plan.

author: John H
content:
Quick post here regarding my oversized losses from Friday's session. I decided I would play WDC, and as we witnessed memory names had a brutal selloff. But more importantly what did I do wrong. I came in simply unprepared. WDC is not a ticker I normally trade. I had zero premarket prep. No key levels to watch and no exit plan when the trade turned against me. I failed to check a higher time frame to see that we were selling off. I should have marked off the open high low on the 5 min. candle as is often discussed here and I didn't do that either. So, an entire solid month of very good trading cost me more than 1/2 my gains for January. 

To make matters worse, I continued to trade. While I won most of those trades, I took yet another big loss on SNDK. I entered very late chasing more downside and the stock reversed near end of day. I'm saying this as a reminder to myself and for others that might be newer traders how very important it is to map out your game plan. To stay disciplined. To know your exit point if the trade turns against you. To not overtrade by taking too many trades, or revenge trade to try and recoup losses. Had I followed <@1014389514141184074> Friday advice I would of had a much better week & month. I was up $42K for January, I ended with $16K. I was disciplined all month long until Friday. That is truly how quickly you can make careless errors and give it all back. 

I still have a very high win rate, but you'll find that does not matter if you let trades get worse by the minute and don't get out when they are clearly not working. I will come to the market prepared, or I will not trade. That is something I have to follow for myself. We ride again Monday, and you all will see me turn this around. Best of luck everyone.

author: Luke
content: Hey <@293563891345129472>, I really like the trade based on a "5min setup." As far as momentum on the 1min timeframe, you would like to have seen it work out right away rather than consolidating. After about 10:12am EST, I would have not been interested in it anymore based on the 1min timeframe since you would like to see it reject immediately especially for shorts. This is where you could go to the 5min timeframe if you have more of a bearish thesis and look to enter based off of the 5min timeframe. Also, another reason this would be a "5min setup" (you'll hear Jack and Tony refer to these as that) is you can see that there is a lot of displacement... in my opinion just a little too much for momentum shorts on the 1min timeframe for me, but based on the 5min chart it looks super nice. But besides that, your stop loss looks solid and your entry. This was definitely a trade you could have looked to re-enter with the 84% Rule on. Nice trade though!!

author: Joebag009
content:
The numbers: Cash account +64  Margin account:  -400
Take Profit Trader eval: -162 Top Step eval: -283 Apex evals: No trades

The Story
Lots of learning today. I’m not doing well personally, or in trading at the moment. Spilling my guts on here in a narrative others can comprehend instead of just short form writing to myself might help me work through it. I have done much better before so I know I can do this. 

Cash account: I have been trading in a margin account and sizing up worked great until my slump, since I have been trading 1-3 cons lately, I figured why not try a cash account with no margin? I took 720 bucks and put it in a cash account. I did one quick trade with JDUB on 2 0 day cons of NVDA and made 64 bucks. I could have held longer for continuation – I have gotten better, but still hold losers longer than winners. Losses happen faster than winners, cutting needs to be ruthless. I got lots of insight so my new rule for next week is trade 1 account only – cash if I can, margin if the trade of the day needs more capital. 

Margin account: I then had a thesis of continuation and a quick win in my margin account, but instead of waiting for a break and retest of that low of day on NVDA I entered too soon and it turned out being low of day. I stopped out on 2 NVDA 0 days for a loss of 314. Re-entering with hope of continuation and being wrong is something I am seeing as a problem. I will need to stick to the original setup and once I’m out, I’m out. If it costs me, I need to get better at exiting, instead of trying to re-enter. I also need to keep in mind 0 days move different. Looking at Fridays for the last 3 months I don’t see a meaningful performance difference from other days, so for now I’ll keep trading them.

author: Joebag009
content:
I took a quick apple scalp since JDUB was looking at it and bought two 0 day cons. One sold at profit of 17 and one tight stop out at -9. They reversed shortly after so my exit was ok. Then I saw NVDA come back within 5 bucks of where I entered. It retested and I tried again with Jack and chat talking about continuation (but not high conviction on retrospect, it didn’t close under the level I was looking at with conviction and the market reversed). While I was wrong on the setup I did manage risk by switching from 0 days to next Friday expiry. I had a tighter stop but sized up like an idiot to 3 cons instead of sticking to 1 on a lower probability setup and overall should have stayed out because it is Friday and my 2nd attempt at NVDA already failed. Looking back it felt rational but was an emotional trade. I lost another 118 in my margin account stopping out. 

I took a solo trade going long on QQQ when it popped above the 15 minute orb and defended it with the 10:31 candle, it did not continue higher so I sold my single 0 day con for a profit of $33.00 very quickly. This turned out to be a smart decision as it didn’t break higher for two more hours, by then most would have stopped out and any contract would have decayed.

I gave spy long a try but had an ultra tight stop and was done at -9 bucks, also wise as it rejected that level about 10 times and then failed bigly hours later.

author: Joebag009
content: I had a big AHA that if it was my cash account I never would have risked subsequent NVDA trades, so I think I might be trading my cash account with 720 in it (and taking profit out each day) for a few days to build my psychology up with a fresh start. Another big aha is that with 0s and even generally the first trade is the best trade and unless I have a new thesis or proved continuation (preferably with divergence from the market), re-entering is a bad idea - looking at my trading notes I have had wins in the past on re-entering for continuation, especially in big dumps around key levels and in grinds but this week it has not worked out (as in net profit) and eaten my meagre profits. Instead of blaming the market, I am adding new habits of pausing between trades and to reload I will develop a quick checklist for myself - did it defend a key level and continue or is it just teasing me, or is it just teasing me as it mirrors qqq moves (hey, it got Jack with TSLA this week, I need to be kind to myself, but also prevent it next time), is it over-extended on the stock, and with qqq overall? What is the daily tf trend? Are Jack and Tony still in and either not at target or just tp1? Tp2 and “to the moon” are horrible places for re-entry, even if it is holding a key level or 1 minute timeframe. Reminder to myself that re-entries pay less, fail more often, and lose money more quickly than initial setups.

author: Joebag009
content:
Jack and Tony signed off for the day so I switched my attention to FlowZone on Youtube my Take Profit Trader (TPT) and Topstep evals with mnq micros. Lots of trades where take profit and stop were within a range of 20 bucks to get out of the trade as the scalp setup failed. On TPT my eval is almost blown from being on tilt the other day, I went from 248 of wiggle room down to 104 so I paused on that account and hope to trade back on an A+ setup later or on a different day. I was careful and followed my rules, just had the trade go against me. I tried one more trade later and was wrong, down to 86 in the eval, maybe I’ll get lucky next week and build it back up. If I blow it I will focus on my other evals. I tried a few props and the number I have now is too much to trade. On topstep I ground to +150 then down to 50 then ranged 60 to 120 and took a pause as the market just couldn’t decide what it wanted and scalping volatility died down and I wanted to protect my hard won gains. I then broke my rule of being done when the streamer is and went on tilt, shut off the chart at -283 so I can live to fight next week.

author: Joebag009
content:
I keep getting to the half way point of passing evals and then getting close to blowing them, grinding up, then either failing with tight margin or a second tilt day. I wish I had tried props sooner, days of learning only costs me the eval fee. I wish more that I was passing them. I want to try trading multiple accounts at the same time with a copy trader for base hits that add up, I have two Apex accounts and wanted to link them to Ninja trader, only to find out that the feature was removed with the latest update to Ninja 8. I might try trading both together on Rthmic trader pro, but haven’t figured out the right settings and also need to tweak the master settings to switch from Ninja to Rthmic. I was super exited for Ninja with all the flashy Youtube shorts out there, it looks like ass out of the box and I am let down. So far my best results have been with TPT. Apex trailing drawdown scares me, I need 1 solid trade per day which might be good for my trading, but if I trail too close I’ll get stopped out in profit and then if I try to re-enter I’ll get cooked on small moves and spreads. If I size up I can’t give it too much room without blowing the account. Two micros and one trade attempt per day might be the best way to go, but how much is a healthy stop and healthy trail for Apex. I think Flowzone has a video on Apex best trading practice so I’ll check it out. I need to start trading the accounts next week so I can pass before they renew but this week has sucked and I’m not starting on a Friday.

Between all my accounts my challenges are: overtrading, tilt, sizing up instead of being consistent, not differentiating between good and crap setups, and rule breaking by staying on charts too long.

author: Joebag009
content:
Prop accounts: TPT -about 30 and 50 cents away from blowing it, will try a trade but fully expect to lose it and not planning to renew – will focus on my other accounts now that I have ruled out trading outside NY session.

TopStep: no trades.

Apex 02: +105 followed rules well
Apex 01: -264 slightly overtraded but caught myself and closed the charts before it got worse

Elite athletes don’t rise to the occasion; they fall to their level of training. -Neto Moreno

Theme of the day and Aha #1: SLOW THE HELL DOWN, be patient, DO NOT LONG OR SHORT IN CHOP, AT EDGE OF RANGES, AT HIGH OR LOW OF DAY… so in other words looking at a 1/5/15 min TF of a daily chart, the move looks HUGE, it makes us GREEDY, it makes us FEEL LIKE A BAD TRADER for “only” capturing xyz… but really, “perfect” is timing to SAFELY capture a relatively TINY part of that move with HIGH expectancy and CONSISTENCY. Doing it with 0s like JT (Jack and Tony) grants extra rewards.

Big aha for me today is how much more 0s can move compared to Friday expiries, 80% vs 20 profit roughly, however 0s are sooo spicy, even 1 option feels so high risk, definitely with the low discipline I have had lately. So, I could do 1 con 0 and be out when in profit, no re-entries ever on 0s, just with longer expiries, and not to mention that I’m super cooked if JT are wrong. I feel like I am at the beginning of my trading journey again, learning how to execute accurately and repeatedly with a single 0 will be a new challenge for me, I understand these are just for the main trade of the day and an A+ setup, I will want more wiggle room on most days until I’m consistently green.

author: Joebag009
content:
Big aha with FZ today is that just like with JT I overtraded and didn’t quite match his execution. My goal today was to trade less and more focused on his stops and setups. While I missed some of the moves and botched the execution a bit, I at least paid attention to him with a new type of mindset and followed through and am up in 1 out of two Apex accounts. I could feel my frontal cortex straining, doing REPS, as I breathed through my emotions and focused on market structure. I stayed OUT of MULTIPLE trades, that WOULD NOT HAVE WORKED. So that was a huge psych win for me today, which I need cause I feel like I’m on the cusp of either making it big or completely losing my mind. FZ did 3 trades today, I did more with less size, same pattern as with JT and re-entries, but a bit easier managed on the charts so less friction. 

12:30 PM Eastern behaviour of futures seems to change a bit and most consistent pros are done here if not a lot sooner. Big AHA: a price move on a chart is not equal, cons and futures move different depending on price volume and market structure. “d’uh” but seeing it in real time is different than seeing it in a lesson. A “choppy/grindy” move in both futures and contracts is a lot crappier than a clean smaller move with strong volume and direction. Again “d’uh”, but starting to see it is making a big difference in my emotions and execution. Also after 12:30 fills get worse on stopping out and taking profits making it harder to stack up wins that recover from even small losses (because bad fills make them even 3x worse than AM exit with the same distance stop). In other words, what the chart shows is a lie, the risks of extracting money from what you see go UP.

author: Joebag009
content: Nothing wrong with taking your time, I did longs only for the first 6 months of trading, you can just look at the charts in real time and take notes of what you would have done to help learn and then dip your toes in with 1 con short, even if you typically do more long, and aim for tp1 or tp2 - not full target with 1 con

author: Kam
content:
I’d probably look at the 9:42 candle after the hammer at the 5m OR as the best retest. It’s aggressive (ahead of the 1m OB), but it has confluence with a prior daily level from the prior Fri high and is inline with the 1st pullback method

The 2nd and 3rd pulls look weaker to me because momentum hadn’t really established yet - GOOG was consolidating and the market was choppy. Retests tend to work best after clear displacement & quick pullbacks

IMO the 2nd best momentum retest came later around 11:20 on the 5m once relative strength was obvious.

Big thing though is replaying & practicing these moments in real-time speed. In hindsight a lot of things can look textbook, but we have to go with what we can see in the moment! Just because the 9:42 entry didn't go up & have continuation doesn't mean it was a bad trade according to the system. The 2nd & 3rd entries look good because they went up, but for every chart that looks like this, there's one that fails to the downside also

author: Huy
content:
As many of you have already agreed on this - no more 0's! No ifs. The trade has to work out right away and the move has to be impulsive for 0's to work in our favor. Also it takes another level of mental capital to be able to hold through the ups and downs. Only Jack and Tony can get away with this due to where they are in their trading journey + their experience. My suggestion is only take 0's once you get to a certain point but the size is going to be much smaller. Vast majority of us including myself are not there yet.

I can give you 2 feedbacks on the trade today. 
1. NVDA was consolidating after we got in but it never broke market structure. There was an order block that it was respecting nicely
2. QQQ also never broke structure

do not get me wrong, today was also tough. It takes some mental fortitude to hold through that

author: John H
content:
<@300218937587662849>  I took this trade as a pure momentum play off the open. I entered the trade just as the 9:32 candle was taking over the 9:31 candle. I was in profit right away, stop moved to BE. 

I watch and trade this stock nearly every day. If I had waited for a break & retest the trade would have failed, because it reversed hard. My stop was the low of the candle I bought in on. 

It's been my observation that with the lack of continuation, trades need to be entered earlier. There of course was a nice short immediately after the stock popped, and then subsequent buying again around 10:30. 

So that was my game plan today. Hope this helps.

content: Hi. I trade 0DTEs, so for 0DTEs what works for me is my max loss per trade I set up beforehand. This is because in 0DTE, I need the contracts to move in my favor reasonably fast or I cut it, I have learned that for 0DTE what keeps me alive  and profitable, is small losses and reenter if market structure is intect.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Someone told me that Hyenuk the Korean/Colombian trader who teaches in Spanish applies the rule of 8% of his contracts size for everything, either swings or intraday. This is so with slippage and your own reluctance to exit will never become more than 10% of your contract size that loss. People forget that we can always re-enter. Commissions are low, and unless you trade 50% of your account per trade, you can always re-enter. 
Never forget this, you can always re-enter. It took me almost 4 years to understand and apply this simple notion to become profitable. Sometimes, the obvious things are realized late and very very costly.

author: 305 Trader
content: ok. The displacement refers to the move made by the stock in your direction prior your entry. You would want a RvR measured from your stop loss to that top of the displacement of 1:1. Then your ultimate and messured final take profit be of 1:3 RvR  or more from your entry.  Can you mark the candle in which you entered? To be 100% sure of what you say.

author: JP400
content:
The creamy area is basically the "fill in" between the 5m ORH and ORL. I could turn it off. I choose to have it on. The dark Green solid line is PDH. The paler Green solid line is PDH from previous 2 or 3 days. The large dashed Green line is PMH. The smaller dashed Green line is PWH.
There is every possibility that I am making the mistake of having too many levels drawn on my chart.

Sometimes I think I should stick with 5m ORH/L or PDH/L or PMH/L.
I may even turn off the 5m ORH/L altogether since it seems a little random to base entries on 5m (why not 6m or 7m or 10m or 15m). At least the PD levels and the PM levels and the PW levels are following levels that seem... less random.

Thanks for your patience and perseverance ! 🙂

author: Martin_AT
content:
One day later I looked at the trade again and I found out the mistake I made and why it confused me. Simply the entry was too late. I make this mistake from time to time and this usually leads to a bad RWR. So when something like yesterday happens my SL gets triggered and then price moves to TP.
So the logical "solution" for me is to really focus on entries very close to the level and if the trade is already gone not tho chase the trade anymore.
In the screenshot you can see that if you just move the Long-Tool a bit lower then the trade would have worked out. So simple in hindsight but sometimes very hard to understand during the "hectic" trading session. Therefore will adapt my "daily remainder" so that I can avoid this in the future.
Maybe you had the same issue and this helps you as well

author: nasman
content:
Hey everyone, wanted to share a transparent update. I’ve officially wiped the $1k I started with.

This wasn't a failure of the strategy or the course, it was 100% my execution. My journal shows a "death by a thousand cuts" from:

- FOMO & Chasing: Entering late and forcing setups.
- IBKR slips / accidental execution
- Revenge Trading: Trying to "claw back" small losses and emotionally reacting.
- Stop Loss Management: I honored my stops, but didn't adjust/edit them properly for the price action.

Fortunately I only traded one contract and kept my position size small at all times. 

I’m stepping back to reset and build back the right way while trying to refine my edge. 

For the mentors and experienced traders:
1.    How do you mentally reset after a wipeout to avoid a "deficit mindset" on the next trade? I do see myself holding biases from losses. 

2.    What’s your best drill for rebuilding entry discipline?

Appreciate any feedback. Back to work!

author: 305 Trader
content: Hey JP, I dont know if you are a woman or a man, i just wanted to tell you that I felt both laugh and compassion with your message. Laugh because you said “we throw the brain out of the window”. As for the rest, sounds like you have tried many things but the one thing still eludes you is the adhering to simple rules after observing what works. The only answer is: keep trying to figure it out for your self. But, with only 1x con. No more. Forget about the notion of getting rich or making a living out of this. Just sit down and trade with just 1x con, adjust your entries. The market has turned difficult for the day trader (not the swing trader with capital to hold). Corrections are very brief, ranges are long for some symbols. so go long for the most part. I also had a bad probably worst month of the year in May. Also, because I departed from what made me profitable in 2026 to try a new strategy which proved it is not working in this market with the Elliott waves I was trying. So, what we have to do is to adhere to a set of 3-4 rules and pick up our brains from the garbage can and force it to sit, “just to sit”and to wait for the rules to present themselves in the form of a set up and hit it. But also to act early as soon as they are spotted, this is the flow, to be able to cruise in low speed for a while but to switch gear quickly when the rules present themselves or viceversa. There it lies the fluidity of the same day trader.

author: Martin_AT
content:
Hello,
on Friday my overall bias was correct because my bias was to the downside. QQQ dropped after market open and was below PDL but I still hesitated to go short on AMD. I missed the first two order block retest opportunities. On the third order block retest I already had a bad feeling but I still went short. The trade was a looser but after 11:10 am price continued to go down.
https://www.tradingview.com/x/9jDs9Idd/
For me the learning is this (more like a "re-learning" because I should have known this): If Qs is dropping for about 30min without a significant pullback then the chance for a bigger pullback or a reversal is too high and therefore shorting stocks on the 1min TF is too high risk. Thus in future I would focus on shorting opportunities at the beginning of the session and avoid late short opportunities like AMD von Friday. (Based on all the trade reviews from Tony/Jack that I have watched I noticed that they never go short too late - like me in this case)

What are your thoughts on this? Do guys also avoid late entries or is there something else I missed? Thanks

author: Jdub
content: Yes. When trading options espically right off of the open it can be hard to manage risk sometimes becuase price moves so quick. The easiest way is to look at the options chart real quick to give you an idea of how much your risking. If price is consolidating and you have more time you can calculate the risk amount with delta for a more precise risk amount. That's why when you risk a same amount per trade you understand lets say for example tsla I only need to give it 10% to know if I'm right or wrong. So if I usually risk 10% that means I will risk $750-$1000. That means my posistion size will be anywhere between $7500-10,000 position size depedning on the price of the contracts.

author: BackwoodsDisco
content:
I made a google spreadsheet that calculates it all, based on account size and how much I want to risk each trade. Once I used it for a while, I got the general idea and do quick math in my head
Say you have 20k and you want to risk 3% on each trade. That’s $600 risk per trade. If you set a 10% stop loss, then total contract value would be $6000. So, $6000 divided by the cost of each contract = how many contracts to buy.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: I'd like to share few things. It's been 2 months in this room. And i had my attention glued to mainly learning. Taken probably about 10-15 trades in 2 months😵‍💫  Could have really followed the call outs and made money  🤌  But i can't take something i don't understand. Here's a few things that really helped: Creating a proper playbook with screenshots and entry/exit criteria; Backtesting each setup (proper backtesting, i realized i did it wrong all those years); doing video trade reviews (same as Tony and Jake do on youtube. created a channel and posting it there) Helps so much! Also created a discord where i post my watchlist and my thoughts. Having my thoughts written out helps with discipline!!! Some days i think i didn't make any progress so i made a list of things i learned in 2 months and it's impressive. Next step is getting off the lives and doing my own stream so i can talk through my thoughts and watch my emotions. Will review posted lives for my mistakes and thought process. I really need to hear my own thoughts otherwise i'm distracted. Some people can do 3 things at once, but it's not me. My main message here i guess i can't do less than Tony or Jake and be profitable.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content: I have relevant problems, and I know some other people have these, because it's difficult to manage risk. If you enter with Tony/Jdub, it might be later than them because the video might lag behind. Thus stop loss is wider. Scaling out at HoD makes RR less. This might lead to losing the same amount on losing trades that you gain on winning trades (and no continuation on trailers). If you can't manage to risk each time the equal amount, which is hard because you don't always know the stop loss potential risk when you enter the trade, you might end up red.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hey everyone,

We’re all here with the same goal: to learn and grow as traders. We’re like a family, supporting each other through the ups and downs. I know that red days can really throw our emotions off (it happens to everyone!), but it’s important to keep those feelings in check.

The Break and Retest strategy works, but it needs to be paired with solid risk management, the right mindset, good execution, no bias, and thorough preparation. Let’s keep growing together and focus on mastering these elements.

Remember, it’s all about your trades and your account. Let’s support one another and avoid any “I’m right, you’re wrong” mindsets.

Thanks for understanding and for being part of this awesome community!

author: TheBat
content:
If it can help some people especially the one with smaller account here the things im trying to focus on . 

Today is my first red day in August, but overall this is a board of the habits i'm trying to focus on in order to judge or not if i have a good or bad day . As you can see even if today its my first red day my trading overall is not perfect at all.

B&R Strategy is not perfect, but she is working plenty of people show this everyday. Our main problem i think, its all the stuff that came beside (RM, psychology, knowing when to stop...) and there is only 1 person able to fix it even with the best mentor or signal in the world is ourself. 

Keep grinding, trust and respect the process, Tony said  it take time to change many years of bad habits and behavior
