author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Actually, when to enter and what kind of price action to seek on entry seems to me even harder than finding a working setup. Especially with small scalps to HoD, if you have to enter late, you better skip this trade. 

I rewatched some videos about the strategy recently, and I noticed that sometimes Jdub  enters the trade  upwards on the black candle of retest, without waiting for a green candle to form and show some displacement. Thus the risk is much smaller. That's a thing I want to look into, which entry conditions work better in which kind of trade and market.

Also, during the last month I've noticed (maybe it's just coincidental) that if the trade goes in the wrong direction or doesn't show impulse in the first couple of minutes, most of the times it will eventually exit on stop loss. I'm thinking of cutting the trades that don't seem to work earlier. Of course, there are trades where a wick touches the stop loss and the price goes up and reaches the PT, but doesn't seem to me that there are many of these.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)

You put a trade on with a stop loss, the market cleans you out and rallies back up. 
It's something you have to get used to. It's annoying, I know. But don't let it distract you from your process.

Show up, plan, execute, record mistakes, get feedback, improve, execute again. You got this!

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Hi guys, this might be interesting to anyone struggling with RR like I do. I revised my trades (made during live sessions) of the last month, and here's the stats.

There were 10 losing trades (exit on SL, actually 9, but for one trade I couldn't remember the setup so I counted it as a loss in terms of this analysis), and 12 winning trades. 3 of the losses were on FOMC day, so they can actually be excluded as I shouldn't have taken them at all. But still.

RR of the winning trades: 
9,39
4,73
4,07
2,08
3,29
2,05
4,32
3,04
1,4
3,16
5,18
3,3
That's if I didn't close position until the initial stop loss was hit and just waited until the trend changes or the day ends, and took profit at maximum (which you never know for sure, of course).

So there was only one trade with RR less than 2.
I'm actually red this month due to uneven risk management and selling 30-50% of the position at HoD most of the times (I trade 4-6 cons).

So I made these calculations to understand what kind of result I would have now if I only sold at 2RR or 3RR.
Check the screenshot, it's all there. 
If you trade a small number of cons as I do you might come to the same conclusion as I did, that even closing the whole position at 3RR without taking partial scales at HoD/LoD might have been rather profitable.

Of course, that's a random month and these stats aren't really representative. But I personally want to try something like selling 4 cons out of 6 at 2RR, and the rest (2) at 3RR.

Hope this is helpful 🙌

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
I never moved my SL to BE, I just scaled out 30-50% at HoD/LoD and scaled out the rest at psychological numbers and final target without any trailers. This led me to playing most of the time at RR 1:1, if not worse. That's why I decided to check the actual potential RR of the trades I took.

As for the analysis, you can see above that of my 9 losing trades I could restore, only 1 could use scaling out at HoD/LoD, and the rest of losing trades never reached HoD/LoD. So, if of my 12 winning trades I scale all of them 50% at HoD/LoD, that's 50% loss of potential profit of 12 trades, compared to this one trade where kinda saved 1/2 of risk by scaling out at HoD/LoD.

Of my trades of this month, many times price was kinda consolidating right after the entry, so closing position at BE didn't make any sense most of the time

Like this trade on AAPL I took on August 19th. Most of the time it's like this. The price is indecisive and then it breaks up or down. If I cut the position at BE, considering the entry price, the RR just doesn't make sense.

author: BullBearXInsights
content:
I’ve been part of several trading groups, and every time I joined one, I ended up blowing my account. When I traded on my own, I managed to make some profits. After joining this group, I blew my account again, and I realized it was because of me. I lacked focus and started relying too much on others' decisions instead of my own. I forgot that my purpose for joining the group was to learn from their trading mindset—how they think and trade. Believe me, Tony and Jack are the best compared to other groups out there.

One key lesson I learned was to do my homework, compare my analysis with theirs, and make decisions based on my own playbook. I started trading with smaller sizes, learning how they form their opinions. Interestingly, while I’m now closely aligned with their views, I still make slightly different decisions when it comes to entries and exits. I think these differences stem from their experience and account size. I feel more confident now, but as we all know, consistent trading success requires years of experience—at least three years of learning, and yes, blowing up your account (LOL).

The biggest lesson I’ve learned is the importance of cutting losses. There’s no reason to hold on to a losing trade. It’s like when you miss your stop at a train station—you should get off at the next one instead of waiting until the last stop. I hope everyone learns from their own experiences and finds success in the near future.

author: Jdub
content:
Just caught back up with the chat.

I'd like to give my thought on some of the things said.

Taking signals is not a long term solution. I can promise you that if you solely rely on signals that you will lose in the long term and let me break it down. A great example of this is yesterday and today. Let's say you took the NVDA trade towards the downside yesterday. Most likely you would have sold on the first push towards the downside because you didn't understand the overall context of the trade. This was a higher timeframe thesis laid out in the pre market prep and we talked about this over the weekend as well. If the market is weak we will be look for NVDA towards the downside. And if you didn't understand this, you would have no conviction to hold for the move towards the downside even though there was a very good chance that it would head lower. 

And today for example lets say you missed the trade yesterday and now you got into NVDA this morning. I mentioned that its a completely different setup and that you have to keep a tight stop on this one as its more aggressive since its close to open. Once again if you didn't understand this it would be hard to capture. Price ended up rejecting off PML and reversing. When it came back up I stopped out quickly because I knew that price was most likely going to reverse. 

What I'm trying to say is that the problem with taking signals is that when you lose you end up taking a full R loss and sometimes even more since you will hold past your stop and when your in a winning trade you sell as soon as your in the green. You can't hold because you don't have conviction for yourself since your relying on someone else but yourself.

And that's just the reality of taking signals. That's why we teach to fish for yourselves. You think Neto became profitable by taking signals. He took some of the concepts that we teach and implemented it to his own trading, where he has his own system, strategy and edge in the markets. 

You saw my st

author: crunchybrewski
content: Most of the tickers we trade in this room have enough volume (contracts switching hands) that the bid-ask spread is pretty tight.  For a lot of my 0 day contracts, the bid ask spread is a few cents and there is enough volume to fill my order before the price moves too far in an unfavorable direction. For instance if I buy a contract for 1$ with the bid at .99 and the ask at 1.01. I can buy and immediately sell the contract for a loss of 2 dollars even without anything else changing because I bought at the ask and sold at the bid. tickers with less volume will likely have much wider spreads and so you can lose money even if the trade didnt move or moved in your direction. Hopefully that adds context to Neto's explanation.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY **

If you’re green for the week, only trade with a small **PORTION** of your profits. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

If you’re red for the week, **DO NOT TRADE**. 

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

author: demchy19
content:
Hi Lauren,
I'm not a professional trader yet; I'm still learning, but I'll share my thoughts on your question. I don't think this follows the 84% rule. If I understand correctly, the 84% rule applies when you have a plan for entering a stock at a specific key level (e.g., the 5-minute opening candle print), and the stock tries to retest that level but fails and goes lower. The 84% rule comes into play when the stock returns to that key level and breaks through it. At this point, you just watch the price reaction at that level. Typically, with the 84% rule, the stock doesn't retest the key level—it just breaks through and moves in the right direction.
From the picture below, we can see that the stock has retested the same level twice. This can give you a stronger confirmation that the level is well established and could push the stock lower. There are likely many sellers above this level, preventing the stock from going higher, so there's a good chance it will drop. But based on your take profit and stop loss, you seem to be in a good position.

author: Jdub
content:
Its not really about being upset because as a human you are going to have emotions. It's all about what you do with those emotions. Am I upset when I take a bigger loss than expected. Of course but its all about how you respond and what you do after the fact. 

Trading is all a game about probabilities. I had conviction coming into this morning on TSLA for a continuation higher and guess what it didn't work out. I wanted to only risk around $2000 on this trade and ended up losing over $4000. But what happened next, I didn't do anything irrational or went to do something dumb. What I did was wait for another opportunity to setup. 

We ended up getting in SPY/ES which was a solid setup and is still working out as I type this out. 

As for relying on trading as income I didn't do this for the longest time. When I started out and even when I was profitable I was still delivering packages on the side. I would next recommend someone to do this full time without some sort of income coming in. Having to "rely" on trading is going to make it very difficult no matter who you are. 

Hope that makes some more sense.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Thank you so much for your kind words and for recognizing the progress I’ve made it truly means a lot. The turning point for me was realizing that trading is all about psychological thinking. I had to learn to trust my knowledge and analysis, stay focused, and, most importantly, put emotions aside. Scaling up became much easier once I disciplined myself to stick to my trading plan, even though the setups stayed the same.
I’ve failed many times in the past because I didn’t stick to my plan and let emotions take over. It wasn’t until I committed to following the plan and staying disciplined that things started to change. Your mention of the Plateau of Latent Potentialreally hit home it’s such a powerful concept. There were so many moments when I felt like all my hard work wasn’t paying off, but eventually, everything started to click. Breaking through that phase took persistence and mindset shifts, and it felt amazing.
And no, I wasn’t upset at all by your gesture it actually made me happy! It’s great to know someone else understands and celebrates these milestones with me. Thank you so much for your support and encouragement it truly means a lot. 

Trust the process:)

author: Angel (az)
content:
I can't speak for the pros, but here is my personal experience as a beginner - I'm able to hold the trade and not care because I am comfortable with the risk im exposed to at 2 cons total, I am comfortable with it dropping 50% in value overnight in the worst case. And 2 is easier to manage than 1 IMHO. I don't know how you're sizing but it needs to be what you personally are comfortable with mentally at this point in your journey. I am strict with scaling out at the first pop up which gives you realised gains and it's like a cheat code that makes it easier for you psychologically to hold the second one. The Tesla trade this week for example, my first con scale was 300+ realised, so now psychologically, i am okay with my second con going to -300. (I would not wait that long to stop out, but it lets you breathe and gives you a lot of room mentally). Ever since then, I literally try to not even look at the value of my second con or the price, I am just waiting for the HTF target to hit or structure to break. 

If the trade doesn't work: I believe Jack or Tony said you have your stop losses as usual in terms of trades not going your way, but you have to be more comfortable with a larger percentage drawdown. Stop loss is at where key HTF levels and structures are broken

author: Angel (az)
content: Np 🙂 stay as long as the market dictates since we can't predict when or if it'll actually happen. You just take it day by day and stay in your trailer con until 1) HTF take-profit target hits or 2) structure breaks, and stop loss hits. Case 3 I imagine is expiration day hits and neither 1 nor 2 happened, in which case I would probably reevaluate whether everything still looks solid, and close this week's and decide whether to buy next week's expiration. (Have to check with others on this).

author: demchy19
content:
Usually, red folder news affects the overall market rather than a specific stock. There’s always a possibility that the news will be good, causing the market to go higher, which would also take TSLA higher. On the other hand, if the news is bad, it could bring the entire market down, including TSLA. And sometimes, the news might not affect the market at all.

I’d say trading based on news is a bit like gambling—it’s always a 50/50 chance whether it goes in your direction or against it. Hopefully, that makes sense! 🙂

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Great question! Yes, I prepare a thesis every night based on higher timeframe trends (daily/4H) and intraday setups (15-min/5-min). I identify key levels, plan potential scenarios for both bullish and bearish moves, and focus on reacting to price action rather than predicting.

For your challenge, here are some tips:

Focus on the Bigger Picture: Higher timeframe trends often guide overall bias. Intraday bearish moves might just be pullbacks.
Look for Reversal Confirmation: Don’t assume quick reversals. Wait for signs like volume shifts, candlestick patterns, or break-and-retest setups.
Separate Contexts: Use the higher timeframe for bias and intraday charts for precise entries. Adjust your stop-loss and targets accordingly.
Review Your Trades: Journaling can help spot patterns and refine your ability to switch between timeframes.

For anyone that finds it helpful: 
It sounds obvious to me now but with options swing trading if a trade going wrong and you're significantly red don't hold into the next day even if you know it's going to go back in the direction you planned for. It's better to just take the loss and replace your entry when the reversal signs are showing because time decay will eat up your profits

author: DWC2016
content:
Yea makes sense 100%. But risk and position size are not the same thing and often confused. 

Like Tony will have a position size of $30k but only risk $4k if the trade goes bad which would be %4 risk if the account if it’s a $100k account but if the trade works that how you get those $50k+ days while only risk a small amount. 

Or Ex : You can put on a trade that’s $1000 in size but only actually risk $100-$200 as the stop loss. I think a lot of people get confused between the position size and the stop loss which is the actual risk in the trade. Hope that makes sense. I was confused by this when starting as well 🙏

author: DWC2016
content:
Yea no problem I think this is a common missunderstanding. I traded crypto for about 6 years so about 80% of the concepts transfer over. 

But yea its a balancing act of the position sizing so you can make money when things works and the actual risk (stop loss) your willing to loss. The stop is the actual risk not the sizing unless you dont follow your stop loss rules then you will doom yourself 😅.

But In futures its a bit more straight forward to set a 20% SL but in options you have to do a bit of math. 

but you have to look at where your stop is and relative to the price of the contract cost if the underlying assest moves xyz percentage then the contract to move roughly the same. These is a video on youtube that explain these things. search " how to set a stop loss based on the underlying asset price" on youtube. 

Also if you type on youtube "how to set a stop loss with IBKR" you should see a few videos that show you. Its fairly simple.

author: Royal191

Finding the reason we do or dont do something is the first step toward fixing the problem

author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
I took the put trade yesterday because of multiple confluences. Price had retraced to the 61% fib level which correlated closely to the KL from previous PDH (I read trading bible over last weekend in books channel), PA was showing wicks up for a few bars showing sellers keeping price from breaching, there was a perfect test of the 1min ORB low and I think at the same time Qs and Spy showed weakness. 
I did not expect it to go down that quickly and exited all cons in the same fashion missing out on the huge move downward. I failed to leave trailers and scale. Also exited completely because just minutes prior I was in a call that at peak was 3x what I closed scaling 25% then 75% at breakeven. One lesson is I need to remember to leave trailers. Missed the big move downward. could have been a $3k day instead of $550 if I left trailers. That said I am happy that I am starting to see profitable entries.

author: PewSpatialPew
content:
in general i think of it this way. Each con ATM or 1 strike away, the delta is 0.5 give or take.
That means for each $1 move in the stock, each contract adds about 50$ in value if it goes in the direction you expect.

if you have a tight stop loss, you can size up more cons, and vice versa, a bigger stop loss, you can downsize more cons.

author: PewSpatialPew
content: if you're gonna do OTM, you really need to expect the move to hit your target so the delta works out. else the loss just kinda compounds

author: Moni Leine
content:
Hey Traders,
@everyone

Another fantastic Weekly Recap in the books! Here’s what we covered:

✅ Reviewed this week’s trades
✅ Reviewed mistakes made and how to avoid them in the future
✅ Key lessons to improve our strategies:

- Trade setups: 1' & 5' OR BNR (Opening Range Break & Retest)
- PDH/PDL BNR: Previous Day High/Low Break & Retest
- Key levels: Based on High Timeframe Thesis
- 84% Rule Strategy: a second high-probability entry at the previous BNR point after being stopped out on an initial trade.
- Choosing strike prices for contracts
- Managing stop losses:
🔹 1-BBB (Bar-by-Bar)
🔹 OB (Order Blocks)
🔹 Trailing stops
🔹 Break-even stops
🔹 Hard stops
With the market shifting and volatility on the rise, scalping is becoming the go-to strategy over swing trading. But don’t worry—we’re adapting together!

Jack answered all your questions—huge thanks to him! 🙌

These sessions are packed with valuable insights, creating a friendly and engaging space where our Coach is always ready to share knowledge and help us level up. Let’s keep taking advantage of these opportunities to improve together!

🚀 See you at the next session!

Cheers,
Moni

author: StevenP
content:
ORB-Opening Range Break 
OB-Order Block
PDL-Previous Day Low 
PDC-Previous Day Close 
PMH-Pre Market High
PML-Pre Market Low 
HOD-High Of Day
LOD-Low Of Day
HTF-Higher Time Frame 
LTF-Lower Time Frame
BNR-Break And Retest
SL-Stop Loss
TP-Take Profit
ATH-All Time Highs
TSL-Tight Stop Loss
BOS-Break Of Structure 
CHoCH-CHange Of CHaracter
MSS-Market Structure Shift

author: 𝕁𝕒𝕪
content:
Bro, you lost $30k over 5 years. That’s $6k a year. $500’a month. $125 a week. $75 a day.  

Look at it as your 5 year tuition to learn practice and perfect.  

Are you trading to earn a living. Is it a hobby or are you trading solely to get your losses back ? 

Figure out why you’re trading. You can’t make up 5 years in 1 day or 1 week.  Has it been done in a week or a month SURE. But if we get stuck in a mindset we know is wrong yet are only aware of it after we go against it then the losses are gone and there was no tuition. It was essentially gambling.  

I been there, believe me. I’ve blown $35k in less than 2 months.  I feel your pain. A lot of us do.  

If you make $75 a day and shut her down you will get your losses back in time.  
You’re going to have good days and bad obviously but if your RM is there and you’re actually journaling and going over your journal weekly at least it’s going to make it.  

Anyone can make a trade, we have to find trades.  
All Love Bro. Hope you’re able to get a handle on it all and breathe.

author: Jose96
content: Maybe you’re not sizing up and down correctly based on your stoploss. Usually your SL should be somewhere in the chart that invalidates your trade thesis. Based on your entry, you will sometimes have 20-30cents stoploss, and sometimes 1-1.5dollars. If you want to keep your losses the same, you shouldn’t size the same when your SL is 30cents vs when it is 1 dollar. For example if you are okay losing around 100$ per trade and aiming for at least 200$profit, for a 20cent SL, you could buy 10 contracts (assuming 50dollars per 1dollar move of the underlying, 0.2x50x10cons=100usd), but if your SL is 1dollar you could buy 2contracts to play it safe(assuming the same 50dollar per 1dollar move of the underlying, 1x50x2=100). Both cases, if your stoploss hits you’ll lose around 100$(maybe a little more bcoz how options behave). At least that’s the way i size up to keep my losses small.

author: Elvis
content: i guess but there is no way to know what the con will do since SL is based on the stock not the con, so even today if tesla didnt work the loss would have been 2-3 times the HOD

author: Jose96
content: What i explained was based on the stock itself, but trading the option contracts. Say a stock is at 200 and based on market structure, the stoploss would be a break below 199.8. If you buy calls when the stock is at 200, your stoploss would be a 20cent move. If you buy 10calls, and you assume every will decrease 50$ per 1dollar move of the stock, a 20cent move will equal approx 100usd loss (0.2$ x 50$ x 10cons = 100). It wont be exact becoz of how contracts work, but should be a decent rule of thumb if you buy 1otm contract(40-50delta). If the SL is at 199, thats a 1dollar move, so if you want to only lose around 100usd if you lose the trade, then you would buy 2contracts (1$ x 50$ x 2cons = 100). If you buy the same 10cons for a 1dollar SL, you could lose around 500usd (1$ x 50$ x 10cons = 500). This is just something i use to sort of know how many contracts i’m okay buying for a specific trade opportunity, hope it helps a little

content: Hey. Firstly, I feel your pain man. Gotta look at those higher time frames. Pretty much what Jack/Tony did. But remember they're not always correct in the immediate sense. My experience...patience is good when on the the right side of a trade OR when you're intitally wrong, trust your thesis, and have bought enough time out (like 2 weeks to a month) for you to profit or at least break even. Maybe give it a lil time but you also don't wanna regret not cutting your losses at 3k when you're down $12k. Also think about the consolidation on the Daily chart. So if we're breaking above this level... it's probably gonna move with a little strength. very unlikely those puts will recover.

author: GoodVibez
content: At what Price point did you enter your shorts? This is something that I've kept in mind as I've experience a few big hit and have seen some comrades take some massive hits. This one thing... the market is predisposed to go higher. It's nature. We plant seeds so they can grow. Big Institutions want more, not less. So moves down aren't beneficial in the broader picture. I only enter puts in the short-term sense. IF it hits my stop loss or doesn't move towards my target with immediate conviction... I'm out that trade. I also don't leverage as much when entering shorts. Holding shorts for swings is a no-go in my rule book. I wait for big retracements or breakout levels and buy UP for time out. The likelihood your cons recover with longs/calls is higher. So the general consensus...CUT YOUR LOSSES, before the losses become too large for you to recover from. Can't recover if you don't have any funds to recover with. And the longer you wait... the more theta will eat your liquidity.

author: ma
content:
The key to trading options lies in momentum and volatility volume needs to come in.
If you're trading zero-day options and your stock doesn't move in your favor or just consolidates around  the first 10 to 15 minutes, you're likely to lose premium. Even if the move eventually goes your way, unless it's a big and immediate shift, recovering that lost premium is tough.
The best approach Cut your losses quickly.
Options are tricky. That's why traders like Tony prefer trading during the early market hours your trade usually plays out within 5 minutes. You either win or lose, but you avoid time decay, which is an extra loss that many beginners overlook.

Only consider swing trading if you're truly skilled at reading charts and fully understand market conditions. In today’s market, a single news event can invalidate all your technical levels news plays a major role.

author: Sean
content:
I delt with this alot recently. Slowly starting to over come it, but there are a couple things that come to mind. 

One of the bigger things that helped me was changing my goals. I used to focus on profits and losses. Money. Greed and fomo were getting to me. Especially when seeing others prosper. 

I decided to switch to more process focused goals (i.e. Did i follow my plan, respect my stop, respect my take profits...etc).  Doing so , I think, in time the profits will flow. 

Also realizing that is more about probability.  If you take the same trade/setup every day over a long period of time, you'll win out in the end if you have a proven edge in the market. Backtest & journal. Reflect. Have set max loss per trade and max loss per day, and max amount of trades per day. Implement what helps regulate those emotions in other aspects of your life when you feel them arise. For me, shutting things down and walking away and exercising helps settle things down. 

Hope this helps. 

Don't give up. This is a long drawn out battle against ourselves. Master the mind, master the markets.

author: Bhargav
content:
From $500 to $7,876 – My Journey of Patience, Discipline, and Belief

Hey everyone,

I wanted to take a moment to share something close to my heart – not to boast, but to hopefully inspire anyone who's feeling stuck or uncertain in their trading journey.

I joined this trading community about a year and a half ago. Back then, I was just eager to learn, hungry for growth, and like many of you, figuring things out one step at a time. It wasn’t always smooth. There were days filled with doubt, losses that made me question everything, and moments when I wondered if this journey was really for me.

But I kept showing up.

I studied, practiced, journaled my trades, learned from my mistakes, and leaned on this community for support and accountability. Every win and every loss taught me something valuable – not just about trading, but about myself.

In June, I decided to take on a personal challenge: turn $500 into $5,000. Not only did I hit that milestone, but I ended the challenge with a $7,876 account.

Yes, the numbers are exciting – but what I’m truly proud of is the mindset it took to get there: patience, consistency, emotional control, and unwavering belief.

This journey has shown me that it’s not about overnight success – it’s about persistence, process, and trusting that your time will come if you don't give up.

To anyone in this community who’s still grinding, still learning, still waiting for that breakthrough: keep going. You’re closer than you think.

Much love and gratitude to all of you who've been part of this path. The journey is far from over — let’s keep growing, together. Big Thanks to <@717554586843611217> , <@801169196015943731> and all the Mentors here.

– Bhargav

author: Brute7
content:
<@1148249290293116990>:
The gap line and label in the script are specifically designed to show the difference between the previous day’s close and the current day’s 9:30am open.
It should not be referencing the previous day’s open; the true “gap” is defined as “current session’s open at 9:30am ET minus previous session’s close.”
If the gap label appears at the wrong price or isn’t updating correctly, it’s usually due to chart timing (e.g., before the new session starts) or a possible visualization glitch, especially on non-intraday timeframes.

After the new session opens at 9:30am ET, the gap label should appear at the current session’s open, showing the value “current open – previous close.”

ON/OFF Switches for Every Level:
Easily toggle the display of each key level (Prev High, Prev Low, Prev Close, Pivot, R1, R2, R3, S1, S2, S3, and Gap) directly from the indicator’s settings panel.

Improved Usability:
Show only the lines and labels you care about—customize your workspace and reduce chart clutter instantly.

No Visual Duplicates:
All objects are managed cleanly: turning a level OFF fully hides its line/label, with no overlap or ghosting.

I will often buy an extra week out just to help account for some of the loss due to theta if I see something on HTF, but it doesn't move right away and chops around, but never hits my stop. Yes, buying further out requires a little more of your capitial and puts more of it at risk should the trade move against you w/ lots of strength. Why we have stops though right. But the plus side, is that if you have conviction in your HTF thesis and just feel it needs a little more time to play out, it gives you the ability to hold a trade longer and not have Theta eat as much liquiditiy as fast. This may be beneficial for someone who doesn't have a margin account, has limited funds, and is limited the amount of day trades they can enter w/n a week. They don't have the luxury of opening and closing as many trades.

author: John H
content: I don't think someone else's contract size matters. We have to size to our own accounts and the risk we're willing to take with each trade. For example, your risk might be $100 where Jack & Tony may be at $1000.  So, think in terms of if they bought 50 option contracts, they could only lose $.20 before they hit their max loss. If you wanted $.20 of risk equaling $100 then you could only buy 5 contracts. And, $.20 can happen in a split second. That's why knowing where you are getting out, and sizing appropriately to the number of contracts you can buy is important. Your stop might be at $.50, which would then mean only 2 contracts. Hope this makes sense. At the end of the day, it's not apples to apples to try and compare our trade size to what they are doing.

author: Hayden
content:
- Candle Close Stop Loss

Pros:

Filters out “wick hunts” and intraday noise.

Keeps you in trades that recover quickly after a fake-out.

Helps align with structure rather than random ticks.

Cons:

Risk can be larger than expected, since by the time the candle closes, the option premium may be down much more.

Hard to execute in options intraday because option premiums are extremely sensitive (gamma, delta shifts). A stock moving $0.30 against you can crush the premium more than expected.

Can lead to hesitating, hoping the candle “saves” the trade, which sometimes just means deeper loss.



- Hard Stop at Exact Price

Pros:

Clear, mechanical, no decision fatigue.

Protects capital better, especially in options where theta + gamma magnify losses quickly.

Removes emotional “what if it comes back?”

Cons:

You’ll get wicked out more often, especially intraday.

Slippage can hurt — a 10–20¢ slip in stock can translate into 20–30% option loss.

Misses some trades that recover immediately after the fake-out.



- Slippage Reality in Options

Options don’t move smoothly with the underlying; spreads + illiquidity + IV shifts = exits are rarely clean.

Market orders at stop get filled worse than stock.

Sometimes you get slipped on size even if spreads look tight.

This is why many intraday options traders actually prefer mental stops or alerts and exit manually, because hard stops can trigger at the worst possible premium.


Simplification: If you hesitate a lot on your stops, you have a small account, use exact stop levels. (Manual or Orders) The BNR usually works right away or it doesn't at all so most of the time (In my opinion) its worth it to close out. Tony and Jack have a lot higher risk tolerance than most and have been trading price action for many years.

If you wait for closures - accept the risk you're R/R might be skewed on a lot of your trades but your win % might be higher (if they reverse), better for experienced traders

author: 305 Trader
content: After reading and listening quite a bit on trading mentality, I think I wll keep using a hard stop loss based on a point of invalidation of the trade that I can both afford and that it makes sense to the PA. Mental stop loss, causes pain avoidance and promotes a “hope” which leads to a bloodbath.. We have to think not in on trade but in 50-100 trades. A hard stop loss will keep my losses small while I work on letting my winners ride. Overtime this will lead to a profit factor above 1. This is what matters, not the win rate. If this does not drive my profit factor up (keeping losses small), then I have a problem with my entries and my trade plan that I would need to review but only after my metrics of dozens of trades tell me so.

author: FierceTiger
content:
I am just going to share my trade thesis on TSLA: TSLA is a high-conviction intraday short if it becomes red (i.e. crosses previous day's close) tomorrow or anytime in the week, after this parabolic move up. It is the classic "First Red Day" setup. I think that the best way to play this setup is to short it with everything you got once price crosses from green to red, be out 80% of your position on the first green candle (first consolidation), and leave 20% runners (sell 10% EOD and leave 10% expecting Day 2 continuation). 
Point of invalidation (stop loss) is previous day's close, if it reclaims. But highly unlikely it reclaims on a "First Red Day" setup.

author: FierceTiger
content: It depends on the definition. Some would define "First Red Day" as a break below the initial opening gap price, some would define it as a break below previous day's close. I did short and banked when TSLA broke below the opening gap price today. However, true First Red Day moves are usually larger in % loss and have continuation. I think there will be a more violent move downwards coming.

author: Jerry
content:
Hey guys,

Wanted to share my experience of going from struggling to profitable (just a few months so far, but still). Maybe it can be a good starting point for some of you—it really changed the game for me.
I struggled a lot before. At one point, I was trading 20 TSLA contracts, taking small wins but then wiping everything out with big losses. Ended up down almost $15k in just a few months. That’s when I stopped and decided to really think.
I built an Excel table with all of Tony’s trades this year, split by symbol. Every row was a trade with entry (I added some delay since I’m slower), stop loss, max potential win until SL was hit, etc. I kept adding parameters and I’m still revisiting them, improving execution.
From that analysis, I found the main thing that killed my profitability: scaling out too early. I used to sell 30% on the HOD pop, then more on the next big candle, and my RR was completely ruined.
So I made a script in Excel to simulate different scenarios and find the best monthly outcome. The result was simple: don’t scale at all until TSLA moves at least $4 (around 2R), then exit. That’s it.
That one change completely flipped my trading. Now I trade 10 contracts and make way more than I did before with 20. This is just the first step, of course. Next is working on dynamic sizing based on setup quality, scaling in/out properly, etc. But this shift was the key—it stopped the bleeding and got me back to making money.

Hope it will help

author: Luke
content:
With these trades I scale them purely based on the underlying key levels and not the contract's percentage. How I personally use the contract's percentage to my advantage so avoid unnecessary losses is once i'm in at least 15-20% ish profit (which if you get a move down right away will automatically happen) I don't want to make it a loss so I won't hold to my stop loss. The only time I will hold to my stop loss is if the trade doesn't work out right away. So like at my entry here you can see I wanted to scale 40% at 659, but it didn't hit it and rejected, and my contracts went to around breakeven. If there was any strength after that 10:17 candle I would've stopped out immediately with a super small loss (pretty much breakeven).
As far as the R:R tool goes on the underlying chart. With 0DTEs you can't really base it off that much because they move way differently. Sometimes on the underlying you'll be up 1:1R but the contracts are up almost 2R. One thing I personally will do is if the contracts move to 40% and i'm not at TP1 yet, i'll scale some no matter what because that's like a 2R on the contracts because on these type of trades you should be losing about 15-20% on your losses.
So for your first trade that was up 30%, if I hadn't hit TP 1 yet then I would for sure have stopped out when the contracts hit breakeven, because you can always look to re-enter, so just save yourself from a loss and reevaluate. For your second trade, if it hadn't hit TP 1 yet and the contracts were at 30%, then I would've stopped out breakeven. How I play it is when the contracts are at 40% (2R for the contracts) and price rejects, i'll sell the contracts at 15-20% in profit no matter what so that it doesn't become a red trade.
Those are just some rules i've made for myself over the past weeks of trading thse, hopefully that helps

author: 305 Trader
content:
Here is a really sinple method I use as an options trader for scalping,

a)- What is the max loss per trade I am willing to accept today given my account size?

b)- when the set up is forming, I watch where my TPs are, I take a TP1 and also the ultimate, TP1 to me should be 1:2, the ultimate is whatever it can be. Understand this is a super conservative  (but game changer) approach. 

c) - Here is the science, what is the underlying (real stock) $cents size of the most realistic stop loss (not an arbitrary one)??
d)
Formula: 

 c) x contracts number x 0.5 = a). 

Example : Stop loss measures  25 cts of stock value. 
Max loss per trade is $50
Factor is 0.5
So,
25 x 0.5 = 12.5
$50 / 12.5 = 4 contracts. 

That is your contract size to lose $50 as per your measured stop loss (as long as you exit when it says so). 

then, 

Your RvR  ratio to TP1 is 1:2
So,

You have to measure  and find 50 stock value cents to TP1 to enter the trade.

Notice I measure 1:2 RvR to TP1, yes I know this is conservative, but not really cause you are only interested anyways in a  small stop loss, so provided this is what you look for, a RvR to TP1 of 1:2 is realistic and it will occur often. 

Hope this helps, with this method you will be more focused on the  size of your stop loss rather than % of your account to discard set ups that are not winners in what you need: Lower risk

author: wakefieldcapital
content:
**WHY DIDN'T NVDA WORKOUT TODAY?**

Here's my small thesis, might be wrong but moderators can provide their thoughts as well. While going over the average NVDA move from 9AM to 11AM for the last 1 year, it seems to always be around 1-2%. 
Considering the steep move today (with no green candles) from market open, I feel we have already made a big displacement and the momentum for the first half of the day was almost completed (around 2.5%). Moreover, right below the 5min ORL we did also have the 180 HTF and psych level that did represent a big liquidity zone with buyers aggressively pushing up which led to the retest.

author: Jonathan
content:
All losses teach something. If that NVDA trade still showed a 84% rule after losing a good chunck of % why would you enter again? Personally to me, even if the 84%rule shows itself if my loss has reached a point where i can't mentally handle. I do not enter and just wait for tomorrow. So you not entering that 84% trade can help you depending on how you see it or put into perspective. You already took a big L. Could you have handled another L if that 84%failed? 
Its good to disect every single loss trade and why they failed. 
What was your bias on the past trades you took? Calls or puts?  Ask yourself which ones favored you and which ones didn't. Calls,puts,tickers, time frames, even trading days. All those losing trades mean something about your psychology while trading.

author: 305 Trader
content:
good morning Patel. Do not go hard on yourself. Your value is not defined by 2x losses. Not by 10x or even 20x losses. If you develop a trading plan and you adhere to it, that discipline will define you. Trading is a business where focus on zoom out and zoom in is needed. Fight FOMO, a falling move can also be a falling knife. This particular dump in SPY in its first leg as per your screenshot did not offer an A+ set up in my view for the following reasons:

- 1st reaction candle, too large. Increasing the Stop loss unnecessarily if you like to wait for a confirmation candle.
- your 2nd set up, no proper mitigation, just friction and the entry at LOD. 

“Not everything that shines is gold”, its an old spanish saying. Become picky, selective and forget about the profits that people post everyday, it is your journey and your money.

author: 305 Trader
content: I wanted to give you a heads up on 0DTE. I spent probsbly an hour with Charles Schwab on getting some explanation from the Greeks on my loss. The underlying QQQ moved 50 cts adversely but my loss was 40 cts in that same range.  This means an 80% loss in value to $1 of the underlying value in just 5 mins trade. Charles Schwab says that market makers do not like to explain the reason why DELTA is "undefined" and "non-relevant" their words on 0DTE. They encouraged me to use 1 DTE if I could, becuase DELTA then is "more relevant".  thats how far their help went. So, the lesson is, if trading 0DTE, be very picky with the Stop Loss while it is kept real. Because in my case it was just 50 cts Stop loss but the outcome was unexpectedly at .80 DELTA. Also, the IV goes higher as you further away from the ATM, keep that in mind too. I was on the 3rd slot at OTM.

author: 305 Trader
content: And, this loss was at 09:55 EST so  Theta did not really play a role and the trade lasted only 5 mins. Volume was mid range to low.

author: 305 Trader
content: Yes,  I agree. this is probably the extreme case what I experienced, but since it occurred it means it can happen when someone is in with 50 cons instead of 3 cons as I was. So, best advice, only A+ set ups meaning with a RvR that has particularly a low stop loss which I would not make it higher than 30 cts ETF and real, not convenient.

author: EeveeWinter
content: No worries - it’s definitely something to keep an eye on when trading stocks vs indices - on Monday and Tuesday, the option premiums are much more expensive but are more forgiving in time because of theta but Thursday and Friday the contracts are much cheaper but also deceptive because the theta is on steroids (especially Friday) and your trade basically has to work right away or your loss is going to be a lot bigger than you expected

Sure 👍 

Truth is, numbers alone don’t show the full story. They don’t show the bad trades I passed on, the discipline it took to sit on my hands, or the weeks where protecting capital mattered more than making it. Mostly important is the reasons why 

P&L can create the wrong focus, invite comparison, and push people into forcing trades just to “keep up"

author: Dan Grand
content:
What a great day it was here in the chat 😂
Let’s switch the subject 😉

I want to share how this month is going from my side.
I am BE!

From green to zero — and I actually feel great. Why? Because I feel the progress I’ve made since the moment I found Tony on YouTube and joined this community. I’m still developing my edge, but I stick to my rules. My progress has really accelerated since I got here.

Before I met Tony, I was coming to the charts without really understanding what I was supposed to do. What was my strategy at all? 😂
I was hoping the 20 SMA would tell me which button to press 😂
Now I’m kind of laughing when I think about the early days of my journey.

I went through two mentorships and never even saw their performance… not sure why I chose them. Probably because they were posting charts and adding +$$$$ amounts to their stories every single day. As a new player in the industry, that consistency made me believe them.

Now I understand what I’m actually looking for. I have a clear strategy, and I feel like I’m in the most important stage of the process — the BE stage.
This is where mistakes happen, sometimes good profits happen, and sometimes really good losses happen (meaning even if the trade is bad from an HTF context — which is my weakest point because I never worked on it before — I still respect my SL). And i am barely or even not at all lose money 💰

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
===========================
**RULES RULES RULES FOR FRIDAY**

Seriously, take a screenshot of this and keep it handy. 

**If you’re green for the week**, only trade with a small **PORTION of your profits**. For example, if you’re up $1,000, risk just $200-$300. Even if you lose it, you’ll still end the week green. 

If you’re green but don’t have enough profits to play with, **DO NOT TRADE.**

**If you’re red for the week, DO NOT TRADE.**

Even if you’re green and have profits to spare, you can always take Friday off. Enjoy a 3-day weekend—it’s never a bad idea. 

Remember, **__Fridays won’t make you, but they can break you__**. 

Stick to these rules, and you’ll never end the week on a bad note.

@everyone   

Please confirm you have read this with a 🤑

author: Luke
content:
I agree, it is difficult to get used to quickly calculating risk with the long/short position tool and using delta with it to get an estimate. For me, I always define my risk as a range of price; say I wanted to risk $100-$150 on a trade. Of course with options you can never have an exact amount but you can have a good idea. The delta on the ATM Calls on AMZN today was around .50 delta, and I was looking to risk around .50 on the underlying chart. I would multiply .50 by .50 and get .25 meaning you're risking $25 on each contract. Taking slippage and potentially stopping out a bit below the stop loss, I would take 4 or 5 contracts (risking $100-$140ish).
Hopefully that makes sense, that's just how I've done it and it works well for me

author: Apex Trader
content: Don’t beat yourself up or quit yet, because what you’re going through is super common when you’re new. The move right now is to stop trading for a week so your account stops bleeding and learn/rewatch all modules!  Focus on execution instead of emotions, it seems as if your emotions are severely impacting your trading (module 9).  Journal every trade with a screenshot and a quick note on why you entered, where you were wrong, and what would’ve made it a no trade, because once you can see the pattern behind your losses, fixing it gets way easier and your confidence comes back fast (from my personal experience haha).

author: Anil Ali
content: I keep my stop loss at -30%. I've noticed you can't have tight stop losses because prices are moving too fast at the opening.

author: Angel (az)
content: Officially, the candle close under the low/high of the retest candle or some other structure like the key level would be your stop. I wouldn’t call it discretionary - your stop needs to be placed at a location that is repeatable every single time and then if you notice over time that it’s not working, you know what you’ve been doing. Being stopped out of one or two trades and then watching them take off is painful but it happens. That’s why they have the 84% rule as well

author: Angel (az)
content:
Yeah that’s true - there’s definitely different philosophies on this! So Tony and Jack have always taught us to wait for the candle close when we start out but Hayden did a really great video recently for small accounts where he said for capital preservation you can consider stopping out on touch. 

Personally, for the longest time I’ve stopped out on close but I actually just implemented a hybrid rule recently - stop out on close unless if it’s such a huge momentum candle that it shoots past -1.5R in one go. Then I will stop when -1.5R is tagged. That allows me to hold but to limit the tail-end risk to my RR. And it prevents situations like that PLTR situation I see going on right above ⬆️😅

author: TonyMontana
content:
HOW TO USE UNDERLYING TICKER AS HARD STOP FOR OPTIONS ON IBKR:

1. right click on option position in portfolio
2. select order ticket
3. in the basic tab of the quote panel, choose action (buy or sell), quantity of contracts, and order type (market will execute as take profit or stop loss at market price of option)
4. in the conditional tab, press the add button
5. choose condition Price, next
6. in the underlying text box, type the name of the ticker of the underlying stock (TSLA for example and press enter. this should bring up the security lookup screen. Select Stock (SMART)
7. enter operator and Price. For example, for stop loss, enter the price of the stop and the
operator ＜=
8. press the FINISH button
9. set the logic column to "or"
10. repeat steps 6,7,8,9 entering the take profit price and using the
>= operator
11. Press the "Accept" button in the order ticket quote panel
12. review the trade you just entered in the orders panel to ensure everything is correct
13. press the transmit button

<@&1219020123218645102>

author: TonyMontana
content:
It’s important to remember.

Today is FRIDAY.

Taking a low quality trade today is the WORST day to take one.

Why?

Because:

1- 0DTE, you’ll lose more.

2- Weekend, you’ll think about the loss all weekend no matter how well you traded this week.

So it’s very important don’t be someone who ruins their good week because you “want” to enter a trade!

author: TonyMontana
content:
**$75,000 Week Reflection**

I've never done this but since I didn't take a trade today I wanted to try something new.

When I started trading it was solely for money.

Yes, people will say it's for a noble cause, family, helping people, etc...

No.

We all start trading to make money, this is the reality.

The noble causes we use the money for come after, don't get that confused because I've been able to help many people **after seeing consistency**

However, in this chase we often feel behind.

Some of you may be making $10k/month while looking at my P&L and think "I NEED to do more"

Some of you I've spoken to personally making $50k/month and you think your behind.

Others of you may have just joined and still unprofitable.

At the end of the day, I know we are all here to make money and grow not only as traders but people because trading will truly expose your real self.

With this being said, let's try something new tomorrow.

Let's go into the day (all of us) without P&L on screen.

Remove your profit and loss screen from trading.

So all we see is the entry (if trade sets up) and exit.

We simply trade the chart, no emotions involved. 

If we win, fantastic

If we lose, fantastic

I think if 90% of people add this small tweak in their trading it would help tremendously.

It will show you a new perspective of trading the CHART with no bias.

If you like it, you can do it next week as well.

See y'all tomorrow. 

@everyone

Gameplan February 5th 

Coming into today we do have a mini gap down on the overall markets right back inside key levels on the higher timeframes. What I would like to see is the reaction within the first 10-15 minutes on the overall markets. We also do have some red folder news at 10:00AM. 

Lots of conflicting moves. We have some names that are holding up compared to some names that have bad earnings and are dropping. Really want to focus in a few tickers coming into market open.

author: MambaTrades
content:
What’s up everyone! 👋

Super excited to be here! I’ve been trading for 5 years and went all-in on futures last year.

I’m based in Southern California, and my trading name is inspired by my childhood hero, Kobe Bryant—RIP to the GOAT! 🐍

What I love about futures is the ability to manage risk effectively without battling theta decay. My system uses a 1-candle stop loss, allowing for precision and tight risk control.

If you're a newer trader or working with limited capital, futures are the best way to go. They allow you to develop a strong system and gain real experience without high costs. Once things start clicking, you can leverage prop firms and scale to $10K+ per month.

I primarily trade the 4 major indices (ES, NQ, RTY, YM) but focus mostly on ES and NQ because they offer consistent moves and high-quality risk-reward setups almost daily.

My approach is level-to-level trading based on liquidity and market structure. I’m here to share my system and processes to help you build an edge, develop strong trading habits, and master your psychology. 

Big shoutout to Jack and Tony for the opportunity—let’s crush it together! 🫡 

@here
